days.title: Der Dativ, Wiederholung und Überblick
days.objective: You can recognise the dative case and produce the correct article and possessive forms for masculine, feminine, neuter and plural nouns.
days.paretoFocus: Almost every polite or transactional German sentence contains a dative, so getting these six forms automatic unlocks giving, helping, belonging and liking all at once.
drills: Write six sentences with gehören, one for each of: a man, a woman, a child, your parents, your sister, your colleague.
Take five things in the room and say who each one belongs to, out loud, using a possessive in the dative.
List the dative articles for der, die, das and the plural, then cover the list and say them from memory three times.

items: What are the dative forms of der, die, das and the plural article?
What extra letter does a plural noun take in the dative?
Name three verbs that always send their object into the dative.

activities.title: Der Dativ im Überblick
activities.intro: The dative marks the person who receives something or who is affected by an action. The articles change to dem (masculine and neuter), der (feminine) and den (plural), and possessives take the same endings. In the plural the noun itself also adds an -n if it does not already end in -n or -s.

items.hr: Ich gebe dem Kind einen Apfel.
items.en: I give the child an apple.
items.note: das Kind -> dem Kind

items.hr: Der Film gefällt der Frau.
items.en: The woman likes the film.
items.note: die Frau -> der Frau; gefallen always takes the dative

items.hr: Wir helfen den Nachbarn.
items.en: We are helping the neighbours.
items.note: plural: den + noun with -n

items.hr: Das Auto gehört meinem Bruder.
items.en: The car belongs to my brother.
items.note: mein -> meinem before a masculine noun

items.hr: Die Suppe schmeckt meiner Mutter nicht.
items.en: My mother does not like the soup.
items.note: meine Mutter -> meiner Mutter

activities.title: Dativformen üben
activities.intro: Practise choosing the right dative article and possessive ending.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: 'Ich danke ___ Lehrer.'
questions.explanation: Danken takes the dative, and der Lehrer is masculine, so the masculine dative article dem is required.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the dative correctly?
questions.explanation: Gehören takes the dative. Die Schwester is feminine, so the possessive ends in -er: meiner Schwester.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct article: 'Wir helfen ___ Kindern.'
questions.explanation: Helfen takes the dative, and the dative plural article is den. Notice that the noun has also added an -n: die Kinder becomes den Kindern.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence about der Mann: 'Die Jacke gehört ___ Mann.'
questions.explanation: Masculine nouns take dem in the dative, so der Mann becomes dem Mann.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'My father likes the film.'
questions.explanation: With gefallen the thing that pleases is the subject and the person is in the dative, so der Film is the subject and meinem Vater is the dative object.

activities.title: Wem gehört das?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Someone is asking who various objects belong to, so answer with gehören plus a possessive in the dative.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Entschuldigung, wem gehört diese Tasche?
lines.en: Excuse me, who does this bag belong to?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich glaube, sie gehört der Frau dort.
lines.en: I think it belongs to the woman over there.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und dieser Schlüssel? Gehört er deinem Kollegen?
lines.en: And this key? Does it belong to your colleague?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, der Schlüssel gehört meiner Chefin.
lines.en: No, the key belongs to my boss.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gefällt das Büro deiner Chefin?
lines.en: Does your boss like the office?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, das Büro gefällt meiner Chefin sehr gut.
lines.en: Yes, my boss likes the office very much.

days.title: Präpositionen mit Dativ: aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu
days.objective: You can use the seven core dative prepositions correctly and form the common contractions zum, zur, vom and beim.
days.paretoFocus: These seven prepositions carry origin, transport, time and destination, which is most of what you need to arrange anything in daily life.
drills: Say how you travel to five different places, each time with mit plus a means of transport.
Describe your day in four sentences that all begin with nach plus a dative noun.
Say how long you have been doing three activities, using seit plus a time expression.

items: List the seven prepositions that always take the dative.
Which two words combine into zur, and which two into zum?
Does seit describe a point in the past or a period that continues into the present?

activities.title: Immer Dativ
activities.intro: Seven very frequent prepositions always send the following noun into the dative, whatever the meaning: aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von and zu. Several of them fuse with the article, giving zum, zur, vom, beim. Seit plus a time word means an action started earlier and is still going on, and German uses the present tense for it.

items.hr: Ich komme gerade aus dem Büro.
items.en: I am just coming out of the office.
items.note: das Büro -> dem Büro

items.hr: Heute Abend bin ich bei meiner Freundin.
items.en: This evening I am at my girlfriend's place.
items.note: bei means at someone's home or workplace

items.hr: Ich fahre mit dem Fahrrad zum Bahnhof.
items.en: I go to the station by bike.
items.note: zu dem -> zum

items.hr: Nach der Arbeit kaufe ich noch etwas ein.
items.en: After work I do a bit of shopping.
items.note: die Arbeit -> der Arbeit; the verb stays in second position

items.hr: Seit einem Monat bekomme ich jeden Tag eine Nachricht von meinem Bruder.
items.en: For a month I have been getting a message from my brother every day.
items.note: seit plus present tense; ein Monat -> einem Monat

activities.title: Welche Präposition passt?
activities.intro: Practise picking the right dative preposition and the right following form.

questions.prompt: Which preposition fits: 'Ich fahre ___ dem Zug nach Hamburg.'?
questions.explanation: Means of transport are expressed with mit plus the dative, so mit dem Zug is the fixed pattern.

questions.prompt: Which form is correct after the preposition seit?
questions.explanation: Seit always takes the dative, and das Jahr is neuter, so ein becomes einem.

questions.prompt: Write 'zu dem' as a single contracted word: 'Ich gehe ___ Arzt.'
questions.explanation: Zu fuses with the masculine and neuter dative article dem into one short word, and with der into zur.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition that means at someone's place: 'Wir wohnen ___ meiner Tante.'
questions.explanation: Staying at a person's home is expressed with bei plus the dative, never with in.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'After the film we walk to the station.'
questions.explanation: The prepositional phrase stands first, so the verb keeps second position and the subject wir follows it.

activities.title: Mit dem Bus oder mit dem Rad?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend is asking about your journey and your plans, so answer using mit, nach, seit, von, bei and zu.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie kommst du morgen zur Arbeit?
lines.en: How are you getting to work tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich fahre mit dem Bus. Mein Fahrrad ist kaputt.
lines.en: I am going by bus. My bike is broken.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Seit wann ist es kaputt?
lines.en: Since when has it been broken?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Seit einer Woche. Ich hole es am Freitag von der Werkstatt.
lines.en: For a week. I am picking it up from the repair shop on Friday.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kommst du nach der Arbeit noch mit zu Anna?
lines.en: Are you coming along to Anna's after work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, gern. Aber ich komme erst nach dem Abendessen.
lines.en: Yes, gladly. But I will only come after dinner.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kein Problem. Wir sind alle bei Anna.
lines.en: No problem. We are all at Anna's place.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfekt, dann bringe ich Kuchen mit.
lines.en: Perfect, then I will bring cake.

days.title: Präpositionen mit Akkusativ: durch, für, gegen, ohne, um
days.objective: You can use the five prepositions that always take the accusative and keep them apart from the dative group.
days.paretoFocus: Durch, für, gegen, ohne and um cover routes, gifts, opposition, lack and times, and the accusative after them never changes, so the rule pays back immediately.
drills: Name five presents and say who each one is for, using für plus the accusative.
Describe a walk in three sentences using durch plus a place each time.
Say three things you would not want to leave the house without, using ohne.

items: Which five prepositions always take the accusative?
What is the accusative form of der Wald and of mein Freund?
Which case follows für, and does it ever change?

activities.title: Immer Akkusativ
activities.intro: Five prepositions always take the accusative, no matter what they mean: durch, für, gegen, ohne and um. Only the masculine article visibly changes, from der to den, so most of the work is remembering the list. Um also appears in clock times, where the form after it never varies.

items.hr: Wir gehen durch den Park.
items.en: We are walking through the park.
items.note: der Park -> den Park

items.hr: Das Geschenk ist für meinen Vater.
items.en: The present is for my father.
items.note: mein -> meinen in the masculine accusative

items.hr: Heute spielen wir gegen die Mannschaft aus Bremen.
items.en: Today we are playing against the team from Bremen.
items.note: feminine forms look the same in nominative and accusative

items.hr: Ohne meinen Schlüssel kann ich die Tür nicht öffnen.
items.en: Without my key I cannot open the door.
items.note: the phrase can stand first, and the verb stays second

items.hr: Wir sitzen um den Tisch.
items.en: We are sitting around the table.
items.note: um also means around; for times it is fixed, as in um acht Uhr

activities.title: Akkusativ nach Präpositionen
activities.intro: Practise the five accusative prepositions and the forms that follow them.

questions.prompt: Which preposition fits: 'Das Buch ist ___ meinen Freund.'?
questions.explanation: Für expresses the recipient of a gift and takes the accusative, which is why the possessive ends in -en.

questions.prompt: Which of these prepositions always takes the accusative?
questions.explanation: Ohne belongs to the accusative group. Seit, nach and von always take the dative.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence about der Wald: 'Wir laufen durch ___ Wald.'
questions.explanation: Durch takes the accusative, and the masculine accusative article is den.

questions.prompt: Complete with one preposition, knowing that you do not want any sugar in it: 'Ich trinke den Kaffee ___ Zucker.'
questions.explanation: Lack or absence is expressed with ohne, which is followed by the accusative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The present is for my sister.'
questions.explanation: Für demands the accusative, but feminine possessives look identical in the nominative and the accusative, so meine stays.

activities.title: Ein Geschenk für Papa
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a sibling are planning a present, so use durch, für, gegen, ohne and um.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was kaufen wir für unseren Vater?
lines.en: What shall we buy for our father?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vielleicht ein Buch? Er liest sehr gern.
lines.en: Maybe a book? He really likes reading.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ohne deine Hilfe finde ich nichts.
lines.en: Without your help I will not find anything.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wir gehen einfach durch die Stadt und schauen.
lines.en: We will simply walk through the town and have a look.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut, aber um sechs Uhr muss ich zu Hause sein.
lines.en: Fine, but I have to be home at six.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kein Problem. Gegen Abend haben wir bestimmt etwas.
lines.en: No problem. By evening we will definitely have something.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du genug Geld für das Geschenk?
lines.en: Do you have enough money for the present?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe fünfzig Euro für ihn.
lines.en: Yes, I have fifty euros for him.

days.title: Die Wechselpräpositionen, wo gegen wohin
days.objective: You can apply the wo and wohin test to the nine two-way prepositions and choose the dative for location and the accusative for direction.
days.paretoFocus: This one test decides the case in a huge share of everyday sentences about places, and it works mechanically every single time.
drills: Recite all nine Wechselpräpositionen from memory, then check the list.
Take five places in your home and produce one wo sentence and one wohin sentence for each.
Write four questions with wo and four with wohin, then answer each one.

items: Which nine prepositions can take either case?
Which question word signals the dative, and which signals the accusative?
What are the two contractions of in with the neuter article?

activities.title: Wo oder wohin?
activities.intro: Nine prepositions can take either case: in, an, auf, über, unter, vor, hinter, neben and zwischen. Ask a question about the phrase. If wo fits, the phrase describes a position and the case is dative, and if wohin fits, the phrase describes a movement towards somewhere and the case is accusative. The test is mechanical, so apply it before you choose the article.

items.hr: Wo bist du? Ich bin in der Küche.
items.en: Where are you? I am in the kitchen.
items.note: wo -> dative: die Küche becomes der Küche

items.hr: Wohin gehst du? Ich gehe in die Küche.
items.en: Where are you going? I am going into the kitchen.
items.note: wohin -> accusative: in die Küche

items.hr: Das Bild hängt an der Wand.
items.en: The picture is hanging on the wall.
items.note: position, so dative

items.hr: Ich hänge das Bild an die Wand.
items.en: I am hanging the picture on the wall.
items.note: movement towards, so accusative

items.hr: Wir gehen ins Kino, und danach sitzen wir noch im Café.
items.en: We are going to the cinema, and afterwards we sit in the cafe for a while.
items.note: in das -> ins for direction, in dem -> im for position

activities.title: Der Wo-Test
activities.intro: Practise deciding between location and direction, then choosing the case.

questions.prompt: Which question word asks about a position rather than a movement?
questions.explanation: Wo asks where something is and signals the dative. Wohin asks where something is going and signals the accusative.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct German for 'The cat is running under the bed.'
options: Die Katze läuft unter dem Bett.
Die Katze läuft unter das Bett.
Die Katze läuft unter der Bett.
Die Katze läuft unter den Bett.
questions.answer: Die Katze läuft unter das Bett.
questions.explanation: The cat is moving to a new position, so wohin applies and unter takes the accusative. Das Bett is neuter, so the article stays das.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition used for contact with a vertical surface: 'Meine Jacke hängt ___ der Tür.'
questions.explanation: An describes contact with a vertical surface or edge. The dative der Tür shows this is a position, not a movement.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct article, describing a movement: 'Ich gehe heute Abend in ___ Kino.'
questions.explanation: Gehen plus in is a movement, so the accusative is required, which for a neuter noun is das. In speech this usually contracts to ins.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We are waiting in front of the cinema.'
questions.explanation: Warten describes staying in one place, so wo applies and vor takes the dative dem Kino.

activities.title: Wo bist du gerade?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are on the phone arranging to meet, so switch between wo phrases in the dative and wohin phrases in the accusative.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hallo, wo bist du?
lines.en: Hello, where are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich bin noch im Büro. Und du?
lines.en: I am still at the office. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich warte vor dem Kino. Wohin gehst du jetzt?
lines.en: I am waiting in front of the cinema. Where are you going now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich fahre gleich in die Stadt.
lines.en: I am driving into town in a moment.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super. Der Film beginnt um acht.
lines.en: Great. The film starts at eight.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann bin ich um Viertel vor acht bei dir.
lines.en: Then I will be with you at quarter to eight.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich warte neben dem Eingang.
lines.en: I will wait next to the entrance.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut, bis gleich!
lines.en: Good, see you shortly!

days.title: Wechselpräpositionen im Alltag
days.objective: You can describe where things are in a home or a town and where you are going, using all nine two-way prepositions and their contractions.
days.paretoFocus: Finding things, giving directions and saying where you are is daily conversation, and it all runs on these nine prepositions.
drills: Describe the position of eight objects in one room, using a different preposition each time.
Give directions from your front door to the nearest shop in four sentences.
Practise the contractions am, im, ans and ins in two sentences each, one position and one movement.

items: Which two words hide inside am, and which two inside ans?
Which preposition do you use for something between two other things?
Do you use auf or an for a picture on a wall, and why?

activities.title: Zu Hause und in der Stadt
activities.intro: In everyday speech the two-way prepositions almost always appear in their contracted forms. Am and im describe positions, so they hold the dative, while ans and ins describe movement and hold the accusative. Auf means on a horizontal surface, an means on or at a vertical surface or an edge, and neben, über, unter, vor, hinter and zwischen behave the same way.

items.hr: Die Milch ist im Kühlschrank.
items.en: The milk is in the fridge.
items.note: in dem -> im, a position

items.hr: Ich fahre am Wochenende ans Meer.
items.en: I am going to the seaside at the weekend.
items.note: an das -> ans, a movement

items.hr: Der Supermarkt ist neben der Apotheke.
items.en: The supermarket is next to the pharmacy.
items.note: die Apotheke -> der Apotheke

items.hr: Bitte warte vor dem Haus.
items.en: Please wait in front of the house.
items.note: position, so dative, even in an imperative

items.hr: Über dem Sofa hängt eine Lampe, und hinter dem Sofa ist die Heizung.
items.en: Above the sofa hangs a lamp, and behind the sofa is the radiator.
items.note: both phrases are positions, so both take the dative

activities.title: Sachen finden
activities.intro: Practise describing positions and movements around the home and the town.

questions.prompt: Which word fits: 'Der Schlüssel ist ___ meiner Tasche.'?
questions.explanation: The key is inside the bag and not moving, so in with the dative meiner Tasche is correct. Ins would signal a movement.

questions.prompt: Which sentence describes a movement towards a place?
questions.explanation: Ans holds the accusative and marks a movement towards the window. The other three use am, which holds the dative and marks a position.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition meaning right beside it: 'Die Apotheke ist ___ dem Supermarkt.'
questions.explanation: Neben means next to, and because this is a position it is followed by the dative dem Supermarkt.

questions.prompt: Write 'an das' as a single contracted word: 'Am Samstag fahren wir ___ Meer.'
questions.explanation: An fuses with the neuter accusative article das, and the accusative is correct here because the sentence describes a journey towards the sea.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The lamp hangs above the table.'
questions.explanation: The lamp is in a fixed position, so über takes the dative and der Tisch becomes dem Tisch.

activities.title: Wo ist die Fernbedienung?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are helping to search the living room, so keep switching between position phrases and movement phrases.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo ist die Fernbedienung?
lines.en: Where is the remote control?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vielleicht unter dem Sofa?
lines.en: Maybe under the sofa?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nein, da ist sie nicht. Ich schaue mal hinter das Sofa.
lines.en: No, it is not there. Let me have a look behind the sofa.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Schau auch auf dem Regal neben dem Fernseher.
lines.en: Have a look on the shelf next to the television too.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hier ist sie, zwischen den Zeitungen!
lines.en: Here it is, between the newspapers!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Super. Bring sie bitte zu mir.
lines.en: Great. Please bring it to me.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wohin kommt die Zeitung?
lines.en: And where does the newspaper go?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Zeitung kommt in den Papierkorb.
lines.en: The newspaper goes into the wastepaper basket.

days.title: legen und liegen, stellen und stehen, setzen und sitzen
days.objective: You can pick the wohin verb or the wo verb for a given situation and match it with the accusative or the dative, including in the perfect.
days.paretoFocus: German splits placing and being placed into two verb families, and mixing them up is the single most audible mistake learners make when talking about objects.
drills: Take four objects and say where you put each one, then say where each one now is.
Say three sentences in the perfect with hat gelegen, hat gestanden and hat gesessen.
Sort these verbs into two columns from memory: legen, liegen, stellen, stehen, setzen, sitzen.

items: Which four verbs answer wohin, and which four answer wo?
Which auxiliary do liegen, stehen and sitzen take in the perfect in standard German?
What is the difference in meaning between legen and stellen?

activities.title: Wohin-Verben und Wo-Verben
activities.intro: German splits the verb as well as the case. Legen, stellen, setzen and hängen describe putting something somewhere, so they answer wohin and take the accusative. Liegen, stehen, sitzen and hängen describe where something already is, so they answer wo and take the dative. In standard German the wo verbs form the perfect with haben, giving hat gelegen, hat gestanden and hat gesessen.

items.hr: Ich lege das Handy auf den Tisch. Jetzt liegt es auf dem Tisch.
items.en: I put the phone on the table. Now it is lying on the table.
items.note: legen + accusative, liegen + dative

items.hr: Stell die Flasche bitte in den Kühlschrank. Sie steht schon im Kühlschrank.
items.en: Please put the bottle in the fridge. It is already standing in the fridge.
items.note: stellen is for upright objects

items.hr: Die Mutter setzt das Kind auf den Stuhl. Das Kind sitzt jetzt auf dem Stuhl.
items.en: The mother sits the child on the chair. The child is now sitting on the chair.
items.note: setzen + accusative, sitzen + dative

items.hr: Ich hänge die Jacke an den Haken. Die Jacke hängt am Haken.
items.en: I hang the jacket on the hook. The jacket is hanging on the hook.
items.note: hängen belongs to both families and changes only its case

items.hr: Gestern hat das Buch auf dem Tisch gelegen.
items.en: Yesterday the book was lying on the table.
items.note: hat gelegen, hat gestanden, hat gesessen; the form with ist is regional, not standard

activities.title: Legen oder liegen?
activities.intro: Practise choosing between the two verb families and their cases.

questions.prompt: Which verb form fits: 'Ich ___ den Teller auf den Tisch.'?
questions.explanation: There is an accusative object, den Teller, and a movement onto the table, so the wohin verb legen is needed.

questions.prompt: Which perfect sentence is standard German?
options: Der Hund ist vor der Tür gelegen.
Der Hund hat vor der Tür gelegen.
Der Hund hat vor der Tür gelegt.
Der Hund ist vor der Tür gelegt.
questions.answer: Der Hund hat vor der Tür gelegen.
questions.explanation: In standard German liegen, stehen and sitzen build the perfect with haben, so hat gelegen is correct. The form with ist is regional, and gelegt belongs to the other verb, legen.

questions.prompt: Complete with the verb describing where they already are, for upright objects: 'Die Schuhe ___ neben der Tür.'
questions.explanation: Shoes rest upright on their soles, so German uses stehen, and the dative neben der Tür confirms this is a position.

questions.prompt: Complete with the verb for putting an upright object somewhere: 'Ich ___ die Flasche in den Kühlschrank.'
questions.explanation: A bottle goes in upright, so stellen is the right verb, and it takes the accusative in den Kühlschrank.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The child is sitting on the chair.'
questions.explanation: Sitzen describes a position, so auf takes the dative dem Stuhl.

activities.title: Wohin mit den Sachen?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are tidying up together, so use legen, stellen and setzen for the moving and liegen, stehen and sitzen for the resting.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wohin soll ich die Teller stellen?
lines.en: Where should I put the plates?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Stell sie bitte in den Schrank.
lines.en: Please put them in the cupboard.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Gläser? Die stehen noch auf dem Tisch.
lines.en: And the glasses? They are still standing on the table.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Gläser stelle ich später in die Küche.
lines.en: I will put the glasses in the kitchen later.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo liegt eigentlich die Post von heute?
lines.en: Where is today's post, actually?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe sie auf das Regal gelegt.
lines.en: I put it on the shelf.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und warum sitzt der Hund schon wieder auf dem Sofa?
lines.en: And why is the dog sitting on the sofa again?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Keine Ahnung. Er hat dort schon zwei Stunden gesessen.
lines.en: No idea. He has been sitting there for two hours already.

days.title: Personalpronomen im Dativ
days.objective: You can replace a dative noun with the correct dative pronoun, including the polite form Ihnen.
days.paretoFocus: Dative pronouns appear in the most common social phrases in German, from asking how someone is to offering help, so they buy fluency instantly.
drills: Say the dative pronouns for ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr, sie and Sie without looking.
Take five sentences with gefallen and replace the person with a pronoun each time.
Ask three people how they are, once informally to one person, once to a group, once formally.

items: What are the dative forms of ich, du and wir?
Which dative pronoun corresponds to sie meaning she, and which to sie meaning they?
Why is Ihnen written with a capital letter?

activities.title: Mir, dir, ihm, ihr
activities.intro: Every personal pronoun has its own dative form: mir, dir, ihm, ihr, ihm, uns, euch, ihnen and the polite Ihnen. You use them whenever the pronoun stands for the person receiving or being affected. The polite form always keeps its capital letter, which is the only thing separating it from ihnen meaning to them.

items.hr: Kannst du mir bitte helfen?
items.en: Can you help me, please?
items.note: ich -> mir

items.hr: Das Buch gehört dir.
items.en: The book belongs to you.
items.note: du -> dir

items.hr: Ich schenke ihm eine Uhr, und ihr schenke ich ein Buch.
items.en: I am giving him a watch, and I am giving her a book.
items.note: er -> ihm, sie -> ihr

items.hr: Wie geht es Ihnen?
items.en: How are you?
items.note: the polite Sie -> Ihnen, always with a capital letter

items.hr: Das Essen schmeckt uns sehr gut.
items.en: We really like the food.
items.note: wir -> uns, ihr -> euch

activities.title: Pronomen im Dativ
activities.intro: Practise replacing dative nouns with the matching pronouns.

questions.prompt: Which pronoun means to me: 'Der Film gefällt ___.'?
questions.explanation: Gefallen takes the dative, and the dative of ich is mir. Mich is the accusative form.

questions.prompt: Which pronoun replaces 'meiner Schwester' in 'Ich helfe meiner Schwester.'?
questions.explanation: Helfen takes the dative and the person is feminine and singular, so the pronoun is ihr.

questions.prompt: Complete the polite question addressed to a stranger: 'Wie geht es ___?'
questions.explanation: The polite address form Sie becomes Ihnen in the dative, and it keeps its capital letter.

questions.prompt: Complete with the pronoun meaning to us: 'Das Auto gehört ___ nicht.'
questions.explanation: The dative of wir is uns, which happens to look the same as the accusative form.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The soup does not taste good to them.'
questions.explanation: Schmecken takes the dative, so the people become ihnen, and nicht comes at the end to negate the whole statement.

activities.title: Wie geht es dir?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend is having a hard week, so respond using dative pronouns such as mir, dir, ihr and ihm.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hallo Anna, wie geht es dir?
lines.en: Hello Anna, how are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mir geht es gut, danke. Und dir?
lines.en: I am fine, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nicht so gut. Mein Job macht mir im Moment Stress.
lines.en: Not so good. My job is stressing me out at the moment.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das tut mir leid. Kann ich dir helfen?
lines.en: I am sorry to hear that. Can I help you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vielleicht. Meine Kollegin hat mir zu viel Arbeit gegeben.
lines.en: Maybe. My colleague has given me too much work.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sprich mit ihr. Sag ihr einfach die Wahrheit.
lines.en: Talk to her. Just tell her the truth.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Du hast recht. Ich danke dir.
lines.en: You are right. Thank you.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gern. Und wie geht es deinen Eltern?
lines.en: You are welcome. And how are your parents?

days.title: Dativ vor Akkusativ, die Reihenfolge im Satz
days.objective: You can order two objects in a German sentence correctly, whether they are nouns, pronouns or one of each.
days.paretoFocus: Verbs like geben, zeigen, schicken and erklären take two objects constantly, and the word order rule is short enough to apply while speaking.
drills: Say four sentences with geben and two noun objects, keeping the dative first.
Take those four sentences and replace only the accusative noun with a pronoun.
Take them again and replace both objects with pronouns, checking that the accusative now comes first.

items: With two noun objects, which case comes first?
What happens to the order when the accusative object is a pronoun?
Which object comes first when both objects are pronouns?

activities.title: Zwei Objekte im Satz
activities.intro: Many verbs take both a person and a thing. With two nouns, the dative person comes before the accusative thing. As soon as the accusative becomes a pronoun, that pronoun jumps in front of everything else. If both objects are pronouns, the accusative still comes first. A short way to remember it: pronouns move left, and the shortest word wins.

items.hr: Ich gebe meinem Bruder das Buch.
items.en: I am giving my brother the book.
items.note: two nouns: dative before accusative

items.hr: Ich gebe es meinem Bruder.
items.en: I am giving it to my brother.
items.note: accusative pronoun moves in front of the dative noun

items.hr: Ich gebe ihm das Buch.
items.en: I am giving him the book.
items.note: dative pronoun before accusative noun

items.hr: Ich gebe es ihm.
items.en: I am giving it to him.
items.note: two pronouns: accusative before dative

items.hr: Er schenkt seiner Freundin Blumen.
items.en: He is giving his girlfriend flowers.
items.note: the same rule applies without an article on the second noun

activities.title: Die richtige Reihenfolge
activities.intro: Practise putting two objects in the right order.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct order with two noun objects?
options: Ich zeige den Weg dem Touristen.
Ich zeige dem Touristen den Weg.
Ich zeige der Tourist den Weg.
Ich zeige den Weg den Touristen.
questions.answer: Ich zeige dem Touristen den Weg.
questions.explanation: With two nouns the dative person comes first, so dem Touristen precedes den Weg.

questions.prompt: In 'Ich kaufe meiner Mutter Blumen.', replace Blumen with a pronoun. Which result is correct?
options: Ich kaufe ihr sie meiner Mutter.
questions.explanation: An accusative pronoun always moves in front of a dative noun, so sie comes before meiner Mutter.

questions.prompt: Complete the possessive from mein in the dative: 'Ich schenke ___ Vater eine Uhr.'
questions.explanation: The person receiving stands in the dative and der Vater is masculine, so mein takes the ending -em and comes before the accusative object.

questions.prompt: Complete with the pronoun meaning to me: 'Kannst du ___ das Salz geben?'
questions.explanation: The person is a dative pronoun, mir, and it stands in front of the accusative noun das Salz.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am giving it to him.'
questions.explanation: With two pronouns the accusative comes first, so es stands before ihm.

activities.title: Wem schenken wir was?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are planning family presents, so keep swapping nouns for pronouns and watch the order.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was schenken wir unserer Mutter?
lines.en: What shall we give our mother?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich schenke ihr eine Tasche. Und du?
lines.en: I am giving her a bag. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich kaufe ihr Blumen. Und Papa?
lines.en: I am buying her flowers. And Dad?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ihm gebe ich meine Fotos.
lines.en: I am giving him my photos.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zeigst du sie mir vorher?
lines.en: Will you show them to me beforehand?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Klar, ich zeige sie dir heute Abend.
lines.en: Sure, I will show them to you this evening.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wer erklärt Oma das Geschenk?
lines.en: And who is explaining the present to Grandma?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich erkläre es ihr am Sonntag.
lines.en: I will explain it to her on Sunday.

days.title: Reflexive Verben mit Akkusativ
days.objective: You can use the accusative reflexive pronouns mich, dich, sich, uns and euch with the most frequent reflexive verbs.
days.paretoFocus: Getting ready, hurrying, meeting up and looking forward to things are all reflexive in German, so these verbs run through every single day.
drills: Describe your morning routine in four sentences with reflexive verbs.
Say three things you are looking forward to, using sich freuen auf plus the accusative.
Turn three reflexive sentences into informal imperatives, keeping the pronoun.

items: What are the accusative reflexive pronouns for all six persons?
Which pronoun is used for er, sie, es and for the polite Sie?
Which case follows sich freuen auf and sich interessieren für?

activities.title: Sich waschen, sich beeilen
activities.intro: A reflexive verb points the action back at the subject, and German marks that with a pronoun: mich, dich, sich, uns, euch, sich. In the third person and in the polite form the pronoun is always sich, with no capital letter. Some of these verbs come with a fixed preposition, and sich freuen auf and sich interessieren für both take the accusative.

items.hr: Ich wasche mich jeden Morgen.
items.en: I wash every morning.
items.note: mich, dich, sich, uns, euch, sich

items.hr: Beeil dich, der Bus kommt!
items.en: Hurry up, the bus is coming!
items.note: the pronoun stays in the imperative

items.hr: Wir freuen uns auf das Wochenende.
items.en: We are looking forward to the weekend.
items.note: sich freuen auf plus the accusative

items.hr: Setzen Sie sich bitte!
items.en: Please take a seat!
items.note: the polite form uses sich with a small letter

items.hr: Er interessiert sich für Musik.
items.en: He is interested in music.
items.note: sich interessieren für plus the accusative

activities.title: Reflexivpronomen im Akkusativ
activities.intro: Practise choosing the right reflexive pronoun.

questions.prompt: Which pronoun fits: 'Ich erinnere ___ an den Termin.'?
questions.explanation: Sich erinnern is reflexive in the accusative, so the first person form is mich.

questions.prompt: Which informal imperative is correct?
questions.explanation: The informal imperative drops the subject but keeps the reflexive pronoun, which for du is dich.

questions.prompt: Complete this informal command to one person: 'Setz ___ bitte auf das Sofa!'
questions.explanation: The verb is reflexive and addressed to du, so the accusative reflexive pronoun dich is required.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Er interessiert ___ für Fußball.'
questions.explanation: In the third person singular the reflexive pronoun is sich for every gender.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We are looking forward to the summer.'
questions.explanation: The reflexive pronoun uns follows the verb, and auf takes the accusative, so der Sommer becomes den Sommer.

activities.title: Beeil dich!
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are getting ready to leave the house, so use reflexive verbs in the accusative throughout.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Beeil dich, wir müssen los!
lines.en: Hurry up, we have to go!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Moment, ich wasche mich noch schnell.
lines.en: One moment, I am just having a quick wash.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wir treffen uns um sieben mit Tom.
lines.en: We are meeting Tom at seven.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich weiß. Ich freue mich schon auf den Abend.
lines.en: I know. I am already looking forward to the evening.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zieh dich bitte warm an.
lines.en: Please dress warmly.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja. Interessierst du dich eigentlich für das Konzert?
lines.en: Yes. Are you actually interested in the concert?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Natürlich! Aber jetzt setz dich ins Auto.
lines.en: Of course! But now get into the car.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich komme, ich komme.
lines.en: I am coming, I am coming.

days.title: Reflexive Verben mit Dativ
days.objective: You can switch the reflexive pronoun into the dative whenever the sentence already has another object.
days.paretoFocus: Body care, clothes and buying things for yourself all use this pattern, and it is the difference between washing yourself and washing your hair.
drills: Describe your bathroom routine in four sentences, each with a reflexive pronoun and a second object.
Say three things you would buy for yourself, using kaufen with a dative reflexive pronoun.
Write one pair of sentences that contrasts the accusative and the dative reflexive pronoun.

items: When does the reflexive pronoun go into the dative?
Which two reflexive pronouns actually change form, and which stay the same?
What is the difference between the two sentences about washing yourself and washing your hair?

activities.title: Mir die Hände waschen
activities.intro: When a reflexive sentence already contains another object, the reflexive pronoun moves into the dative. Only the first and second person singular actually change, giving mir and dir, while sich, uns and euch look identical to the accusative forms. German also prefers the article here where English would use a possessive, so it says the hands rather than my hands.

items.hr: Ich wasche mir die Hände.
items.en: I am washing my hands.
items.note: die Hände is the accusative object, so the reflexive pronoun becomes mir

items.hr: Putzt du dir jeden Abend die Zähne?
items.en: Do you brush your teeth every evening?
items.note: du -> dir

items.hr: Er zieht sich die Jacke an.
items.en: He is putting his jacket on.
items.note: sich etwas anziehen; the prefix an goes to the end

items.hr: Wir sehen uns die Fotos an.
items.en: We are having a look at the photos.
items.note: uns is the same in both cases

items.hr: Ich wasche mich, dann wasche ich mir die Haare.
items.en: I wash, then I wash my hair.
items.note: no second object means mich, a second object means mir

activities.title: Akkusativ oder Dativ?
activities.intro: Practise deciding which case the reflexive pronoun needs.

questions.prompt: Which pronoun fits: 'Ich putze ___ die Zähne.'?
questions.explanation: Die Zähne is already the accusative object, so the reflexive pronoun moves into the dative and becomes mir.

questions.prompt: In which sentence is the reflexive pronoun in the dative?
questions.explanation: Only this sentence has a second object, die Haare, which pushes the reflexive pronoun into the dative.

questions.prompt: Complete this informal command to one person: 'Zieh ___ bitte die Schuhe aus!'
questions.explanation: Die Schuhe is the accusative object, so the reflexive pronoun for du appears in the dative as dir.

questions.prompt: Complete, meaning that he is buying it for himself: 'Er kauft ___ ein Fahrrad.'
questions.explanation: The third person reflexive pronoun is sich in both cases, and here it is dative because ein Fahrrad is the accusative object.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am putting on my jacket.'
questions.explanation: Anziehen is separable, so the prefix an goes to the end, and because die Jacke is the accusative object the reflexive pronoun is mir.

activities.title: Morgens im Bad
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Someone is chatting to you while you get ready, so answer with dative reflexive pronouns plus a second object.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Putzt du dir noch die Zähne?
lines.en: Are you still brushing your teeth?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, und dann wasche ich mir die Haare.
lines.en: Yes, and then I will wash my hair.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ziehst du dir heute den Pullover an?
lines.en: Are you putting on the jumper today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich ziehe mir lieber das Hemd an.
lines.en: No, I would rather put on the shirt.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kaufst du dir eigentlich morgen die Schuhe?
lines.en: Are you actually buying yourself the shoes tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich sehe mir zuerst die Preise an.
lines.en: Yes, I will look at the prices first.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wasch dir bitte noch die Hände.
lines.en: Please wash your hands as well.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Habe ich schon gemacht.
lines.en: I have already done that.

days.title: Trennbare und untrennbare Verben im Vergleich
days.objective: You can tell stressed separable prefixes from unstressed inseparable ones and build the present tense and the perfect of both correctly.
days.paretoFocus: German builds hundreds of new verbs from a handful of prefixes, so one rule about where the prefix goes unlocks a huge slice of everyday vocabulary.
drills: Say five sentences with anrufen, aufstehen, einkaufen, mitkommen and ankommen, each time pushing the prefix to the end of the sentence.
Take the same five verbs and say them again in the perfect, putting ge- between the prefix and the stem.
List the six inseparable prefixes be-, emp-, ent-, er-, ver-, zer- out loud and give one verb for each, then say its participle without ge-.

items: Which prefix family is stressed and moves to the end of the sentence?
Why is the participle of verstehen not geverstanden?
Where does the prefix of anrufen stand in Ich rufe dich heute an?

activities.title: Trennbar oder untrennbar
activities.intro: German prefixes come in two families. Separable prefixes such as an-, auf-, ein-, mit- and ab- are stressed, they jump to the end of a main clause, and in the perfect they take ge- in the middle. Inseparable prefixes be-, emp-, ent-, er-, ver- and zer- are unstressed, they never move, and their participle has no ge- at all.

items.hr: Ich rufe meine Schwester jeden Sonntag an.
items.en: I call my sister every Sunday.
items.note: anrufen is separable, so an- goes to the very end of the main clause.

items.hr: Gestern habe ich meinen Chef angerufen.
items.en: Yesterday I called my boss.
items.note: Participle: an + ge + rufen = angerufen.

items.hr: Ich verstehe die Frage nicht.
items.en: I do not understand the question.
items.note: verstehen is inseparable, so ver- stays on the verb.

items.hr: Wir haben den Text schon verstanden.
items.en: We have already understood the text.
items.note: No ge- after an inseparable prefix: verstanden, never geverstanden.

items.hr: Die Verkäuferin empfiehlt den Kuchen und packt ihn ein.
items.en: The shop assistant recommends the cake and packs it up.
items.note: empfehlen keeps emp- on the verb, einpacken sends ein- to the end.

activities.title: Vorsilben üben
activities.intro: Practise placing separable prefixes and building participles of both verb families.

questions.prompt: Complete the perfect tense: Gestern habe ich meine Eltern ___ .
questions.explanation: Separable verbs build the participle with ge- between the prefix and the stem: an + ge + rufen = angerufen.

questions.prompt: Which participle of 'verstehen' is correct?
questions.explanation: The prefix ver- is inseparable and unstressed, and such verbs never take ge- in the participle.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the missing separable prefix: Ich stehe jeden Morgen um sechs Uhr ___ .
questions.explanation: The stressed prefix of the verb aufstehen leaves the conjugated verb and stands at the very end of the main clause.

questions.prompt: Complete with the past participle of the verb meaning 'to receive': Ich habe gestern eine Nachricht ___ .
questions.explanation: be- is inseparable, so there is no ge-, and here the participle looks exactly like the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The train arrives at nine o'clock.'
questions.explanation: ankommen is separable, so the conjugated part stays in second position and an- closes the sentence.

activities.title: Am Telefon
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You talk about a phone call and a visit, using separable verbs and inseparable verbs side by side.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du gestern deinen Bruder angerufen?
lines.en: Did you call your brother yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe ihn am Abend angerufen.
lines.en: Yes, I called him in the evening.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und hat er die Einladung verstanden?
lines.en: And did he understand the invitation?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, er hat sie leider nicht verstanden.
lines.en: No, unfortunately he did not understand it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wann kommt er denn an?
lines.en: So when does he arrive?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er kommt am Samstag um sechs Uhr an.
lines.en: He arrives on Saturday at six o'clock.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut, dann packe ich das Essen ein.
lines.en: Good, then I will pack the food.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke, ich bekomme heute noch das Geschenk.
lines.en: Thanks, I am still getting the present today.

days.title: Der Nebensatz mit dass
days.objective: You can report thoughts, opinions and statements with dass and put the conjugated verb at the very end of the subordinate clause.
days.paretoFocus: Almost everything you say about what you think, hope, know or have heard runs through dass, so this one pattern carries a large part of everyday conversation.
drills: Say five sentences that start with Ich glaube, dass ... and each time end with the conjugated verb.
Write three sentences that contain both the article das and the conjunction dass, and check the spelling of each.
Take three simple statements and turn them into reported statements after Er sagt, dass ...

items: Where does the conjugated verb stand in a clause with dass?
What punctuation mark comes before dass?
How do you decide between the spellings dass and das?

activities.title: Der Nebensatz mit dass
activities.intro: dass links a main clause to a second clause and sends the conjugated verb to the very last position of that second clause. A comma always separates the two clauses. Watch the spelling: the conjunction dass has ss, while the article and pronoun das has a single s, and mixing them up is the most common written mistake learners of German make.

items.hr: Ich glaube, dass er heute kommt.
items.en: I think that he is coming today.
items.note: kommt leaves position two and moves to the end.

items.hr: Sie sagt, dass sie keine Zeit hat.
items.en: She says that she has no time.
items.note: The object stands before the verb: keine Zeit hat.

items.hr: Es ist gut, dass du hier bist.
items.en: It is good that you are here.
items.note: bist closes the clause.

items.hr: Ich weiß, dass das Auto kaputt ist.
items.en: I know that the car is broken.
items.note: dass with ss is the conjunction, das with one s is the article of das Auto.

items.hr: Ich hoffe, dass wir uns bald wiedersehen.
items.en: I hope that we see each other again soon.
items.note: A separable verb stays in one piece at the end: wiedersehen.

activities.title: Meinungen mit dass
activities.intro: Practise the end position of the verb and the spelling of the conjunction.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct word order?
options: Ich glaube, dass er ist müde.
Ich glaube, dass er müde ist.
Ich glaube, dass ist er müde.
Ich glaube, dass müde er ist.
questions.answer: Ich glaube, dass er müde ist.
questions.explanation: In a subordinate clause the conjugated verb goes last, so ist stands after the adjective.

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct?
options: Ich weiss, dass das Kind schläft.
Ich weiß, das das Kind schläft.
Ich weiß, dass das Kind schläft.
Ich weiß, dass dass Kind schläft.
questions.answer: Ich weiß, dass das Kind schläft.
questions.explanation: The conjunction takes ss, the article of das Kind takes one s, and weiß keeps ß because the vowel before it is long.

questions.prompt: Insert the missing conjunction: Ich hoffe, ___ du bald gesund bist.
questions.explanation: The conjunction is written with ss and pushes bist to the end of the clause.

questions.prompt: Put the verb meaning 'to drive' in the right form and position: Er sagt, dass er morgen nach Berlin ___ .
questions.explanation: The verb agrees with er and takes the last position in the subordinate clause.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I know that she has no time.'
questions.explanation: The comma closes the main clause, and hat stands at the very end of the dass clause.

activities.title: Was denkst du?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend guess whether someone will come to a party, using dass in every answer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was denkst du, kommt Anna heute zur Party?
lines.en: What do you think, is Anna coming to the party today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich glaube, dass sie kommt.
lines.en: I think that she is coming.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sie hat gesagt, dass sie viel Arbeit hat.
lines.en: She said that she has a lot of work.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich hoffe trotzdem, dass sie Zeit findet.
lines.en: I still hope that she finds time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Findest du, dass wir sie noch einmal fragen sollen?
lines.en: Do you think that we should ask her again?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich finde, dass wir sie fragen sollen.
lines.en: Yes, I think that we should ask her.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut, ich schreibe ihr, dass wir auf sie warten.
lines.en: Good, I will write to her that we are waiting for her.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Prima, ich bin sicher, dass sie sich freut.
lines.en: Great, I am sure that she will be happy.

days.title: Der Nebensatz mit wenn
days.objective: You can express conditions and repeated situations with wenn and handle the word order when the wenn clause comes first.
days.paretoFocus: Plans, conditions and habits all run on wenn, and it is the sentence type people use most when arranging anything with another person.
drills: Say three sentences of the shape Wenn ich Zeit habe, ... and make sure the main clause starts with the verb.
Say the same three sentences again with the wenn clause at the end instead.
Describe two habits with Immer wenn ich ... , for example about coffee, music or the weather.

items: Where does the verb of the main clause go if the sentence begins with wenn?
Which two English words does wenn cover?
How do you say 'whenever' at the start of a sentence?

activities.title: Bedingungen mit wenn
activities.intro: wenn covers both 'if' for a condition and 'when' or 'whenever' for something that repeats. Like dass, it sends the conjugated verb of its own clause to the end. If the sentence opens with the wenn clause, it fills position one of the whole sentence, so the main clause must begin with its verb, and the subject follows.

items.hr: Wenn ich Zeit habe, gehe ich schwimmen.
items.en: When I have time, I go swimming.
items.note: The wenn clause is position one, so the main clause starts with gehe and then ich.

items.hr: Ich bleibe zu Hause, wenn es regnet.
items.en: I stay at home if it rains.
items.note: Same meaning with the order reversed, and regnet still closes its clause.

items.hr: Wenn das Wetter schön ist, fahren wir an den See.
items.en: If the weather is nice, we drive to the lake.
items.note: ist ends the wenn clause, fahren opens the main clause.

items.hr: Wenn du müde bist, musst du früh ins Bett gehen.
items.en: If you are tired, you have to go to bed early.
items.note: The modal verb musst takes the first slot of the main clause.

items.hr: Immer wenn ich Kaffee trinke, kann ich nicht schlafen.
items.en: Whenever I drink coffee, I cannot sleep.
items.note: immer wenn marks something that happens every time.

activities.title: Wenn und die Wortstellung
activities.intro: Practise the verb at the end of the wenn clause and the verb first in the main clause.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct when the wenn clause comes first?
options: Wenn es regnet, ich bleibe zu Hause.
Wenn es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause.
Wenn regnet es, bleibe ich zu Hause.
Wenn es regnet, bleiben ich zu Hause.
questions.answer: Wenn es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause.
questions.explanation: The whole wenn clause counts as the first element, so the main clause verb bleibe comes next and the subject follows it.

questions.prompt: Which German sentence means 'If you are hungry, we can eat now.'?
options: Wenn du Hunger hast, können wir jetzt essen.
Wenn du hast Hunger, wir können jetzt essen.
Wenn du Hunger hast, wir können jetzt essen.
Du Hunger hast, wenn können wir jetzt essen.
questions.answer: Wenn du Hunger hast, können wir jetzt essen.
questions.explanation: hast closes the wenn clause and können opens the main clause because the wenn clause already fills position one.

questions.prompt: Complete the subordinate clause with the verb meaning 'to come': Ich rufe dich an, wenn ich nach Hause ___ .
questions.explanation: The verb agrees with ich and stands in the last position of the wenn clause.

questions.prompt: Insert the conjunction that introduces the condition: ___ es kalt ist, trage ich einen Mantel.
questions.explanation: The condition clause opens the sentence, which is why the main clause continues with trage before ich.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'When I have time, I visit my grandmother.'
questions.explanation: habe ends the wenn clause, then the main clause starts with the verb besuche and the subject ich follows.

activities.title: Pläne für das Wochenende
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You make weekend plans that all depend on conditions, so every answer uses wenn.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was machst du, wenn das Wetter am Samstag schön ist?
lines.en: What will you do if the weather is nice on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wenn die Sonne scheint, fahre ich an den See.
lines.en: If the sun is shining, I will drive to the lake.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wenn es regnet?
lines.en: And if it rains?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wenn es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause und lese ein Buch.
lines.en: If it rains, I will stay at home and read a book.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kommst du mit ins Museum, wenn du Zeit hast?
lines.en: Will you come to the museum with me if you have time?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, wenn ich am Sonntag Zeit habe, komme ich gern mit.
lines.en: Yes, if I have time on Sunday, I will gladly come along.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ruf mich an, wenn du losfährst.
lines.en: Call me when you set off.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mache ich, wenn ich aus dem Haus gehe.
lines.en: I will, when I leave the house.

days.title: Der Nebensatz mit ob und die indirekte Frage
days.objective: You can turn a direct question into a polite indirect one, using ob for yes-no questions and the W-word for all others.
days.paretoFocus: Indirect questions are the polite way to ask a stranger anything, and they are what you need at a counter, on the phone or in an office.
drills: Take three yes-no questions and rebuild each one after Können Sie mir sagen, ...
Take three W-questions and rebuild them after Wissen Sie, ... keeping the W-word.
Say three sentences with Ich frage mich, ... about things you do not know today.

items: Which kind of question needs ob?
What happens to the verb of the original question?
What replaces ob when the question starts with wo, wann or wie?

activities.title: Höflich fragen mit ob
activities.intro: An indirect question hides a question inside a longer sentence, which makes it sound much more polite. A yes-no question is introduced by ob, and a question with a W-word keeps that W-word instead. In both cases the clause behaves like any subordinate clause, so the conjugated verb moves to the very end.

items.hr: Ich weiß nicht, ob er heute kommt.
items.en: I do not know whether he is coming today.
items.note: Direct question: Kommt er heute? The verb kommt moves to the end.

items.hr: Kannst du mir sagen, ob der Bus hier hält?
items.en: Can you tell me whether the bus stops here?
items.note: ob replaces the question word order of Hält der Bus hier?

items.hr: Ich frage mich, ob das Restaurant noch offen ist.
items.en: I wonder whether the restaurant is still open.
items.note: ist closes the clause after the adjective.

items.hr: Weißt du, wo die Post ist?
items.en: Do you know where the post office is?
items.note: A W-question keeps its W-word, and the verb still goes last.

items.hr: Er möchte wissen, wann der Film anfängt.
items.en: He would like to know when the film starts.
items.note: The separable verb stays in one piece at the end: anfängt.

activities.title: Indirekte Fragen bilden
activities.intro: Practise choosing between ob and a W-word and placing the verb correctly.

questions.prompt: Which indirect question is correct?
options: Ich weiß nicht, ob er kommt.
Ich weiß nicht, ob kommt er.
Ich weiß nicht, ob er kommen.
Ich weiß nicht, kommt ob er.
questions.answer: Ich weiß nicht, ob er kommt.
questions.explanation: After ob the order is subject and then the conjugated verb at the end, never the question order verb before subject.

questions.prompt: You want to know the departure time. Complete: Kannst du mir sagen, ___ der Zug abfährt?
questions.explanation: ob only works for yes-no questions, and here you are asking for a time, so the W-word stays.

questions.prompt: Rebuild the question 'Ist das Museum am Montag geschlossen?' politely: Ich frage mich, ___ das Museum am Montag geschlossen ist.
questions.explanation: A yes-no question becomes a subordinate clause introduced by this conjunction, with the verb at the end.

questions.prompt: Complete with the verb meaning 'to cost': Weißt du, wie viel die Fahrkarte ___ ?
questions.explanation: The verb agrees with die Fahrkarte and closes the indirect question.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I do not know whether he has time.'
questions.explanation: ob introduces the yes-no question and hat takes the final position.

activities.title: Am Schalter
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You ask an employee several polite indirect questions about your train.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag, kann ich Ihnen helfen?
lines.en: Hello, can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, können Sie mir sagen, ob der Zug nach Köln pünktlich ist?
lines.en: Yes, can you tell me whether the train to Cologne is on time?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Er hat zehn Minuten Verspätung.
lines.en: It is ten minutes late.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wissen Sie auch, von welchem Gleis er abfährt?
lines.en: Do you also know which platform it leaves from?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Von Gleis fünf. Möchten Sie wissen, ob es noch Plätze gibt?
lines.en: From platform five. Would you like to know whether there are still seats?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja bitte, und ich frage mich, ob ich reservieren muss.
lines.en: Yes please, and I wonder whether I have to reserve.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nein, das müssen Sie nicht.
lines.en: No, you do not have to.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke schön, jetzt weiß ich, was ich machen muss.
lines.en: Thank you, now I know what I have to do.

days.title: deshalb, darum und deswegen
days.objective: You can state a consequence with deshalb, darum or deswegen and keep the verb in second position with the subject after it.
days.paretoFocus: Giving a reason and then its result is the backbone of explaining yourself, and this is the word order learners get wrong most often after they learn weil.
drills: Say three sentences of the shape Ich bin ... , deshalb ... and check that the verb comes directly after deshalb.
Take the same three sentences and rebuild them with weil, noticing that the verb now moves to the end.
Swap deshalb for darum and then for deswegen in one of your sentences and confirm the meaning stays the same.

items: What stands directly after deshalb, the subject or the verb?
Which three words all mean 'therefore' here?
How does the word order after weil differ from the word order after darum?

activities.title: Die Folge ausdrücken
activities.intro: deshalb, darum and deswegen mean the same thing, 'therefore', and you can use them interchangeably. They are not conjunctions but adverbs, so they occupy the first position of a main clause. That means the conjugated verb has to come second and the subject slides in behind it. Compare this with weil, which is a real conjunction and pushes the verb to the end.

items.hr: Ich bin müde, deshalb bleibe ich zu Hause.
items.en: I am tired, so I am staying at home.
items.note: Order after deshalb: verb, then subject.

items.hr: Es regnet, darum nehme ich den Regenschirm mit.
items.en: It is raining, so I am taking the umbrella with me.
items.note: darum is a simple synonym of deshalb, and mitnehmen still sends mit to the end.

items.hr: Der Bus war voll, deswegen bin ich zu Fuß gegangen.
items.en: The bus was full, so I walked.
items.note: In the perfect the auxiliary bin takes second position after deswegen.

items.hr: Ich habe keinen Hunger, deshalb esse ich nichts.
items.en: I am not hungry, so I am not eating anything.
items.note: esse comes before ich because deshalb already fills position one.

items.hr: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich müde bin.
items.en: I am staying at home because I am tired.
items.note: Contrast: weil sends bin to the end, deshalb never does.

activities.title: Grund und Folge
activities.intro: Practise the inverted word order that these three adverbs force.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
options: Ich habe kein Geld, deshalb ich kaufe nichts.
Ich habe kein Geld, deshalb kaufe ich nichts.
Ich habe kein Geld, deshalb nichts kaufe ich.
Ich habe kein Geld, deshalb ich nichts kaufe.
questions.answer: Ich habe kein Geld, deshalb kaufe ich nichts.
questions.explanation: deshalb takes position one, so the verb kaufe must be the second element and ich follows it.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct continuation: Es ist spät, ___
options: deswegen gehe ich jetzt nach Hause.
deswegen ich gehe jetzt nach Hause.
weil ich gehe jetzt nach Hause.
deswegen nach Hause ich gehe jetzt.
questions.answer: deswegen gehe ich jetzt nach Hause.
questions.explanation: After deswegen the verb comes immediately, and weil would need the verb at the end anyway.

questions.prompt: Put the verb meaning 'to go' in the right place and form: Ich habe Kopfschmerzen, deshalb ___ ich heute nicht ins Kino.
questions.explanation: The verb stands directly after the adverb of consequence and before the subject.

questions.prompt: Insert a word meaning 'therefore': Der Laden ist geschlossen, ___ fahre ich in die Stadt.
questions.explanation: All three adverbs work here, and each one forces the verb fahre into second position.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am tired, therefore I am going to bed early.'
questions.explanation: The second clause is a full main clause, so after deshalb come gehe and then the subject ich.

activities.title: Kommst du mit?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You turn down an invitation and give the consequences with deshalb, darum and deswegen.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kommst du heute Abend mit ins Kino?
lines.en: Are you coming to the cinema with me tonight?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe morgen einen Test, deshalb bleibe ich zu Hause.
lines.en: I have a test tomorrow, so I am staying at home.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schade, der Film soll gut sein.
lines.en: What a shame, the film is supposed to be good.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich bin auch sehr müde, darum gehe ich früh ins Bett.
lines.en: I am also very tired, so I am going to bed early.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und am Samstag? Da hast du frei.
lines.en: And on Saturday? You are off then.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Samstag arbeite ich nicht, deswegen habe ich Zeit.
lines.en: I am not working on Saturday, so I have time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super, dann gehen wir am Samstag.
lines.en: Great, then we will go on Saturday.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Einverstanden, ich rufe dich vorher an.
lines.en: Agreed, I will call you beforehand.

days.title: Das Präteritum der Modalverben
days.objective: You can talk about what you could, had to, wanted to or were allowed to do, using the simple past of the modal verbs.
days.paretoFocus: Modal verbs are the one group that keeps the simple past even in everyday speech, so these six forms are used constantly and cannot be replaced by the perfect.
drills: Say the six forms konnte, musste, durfte, wollte, sollte and mochte out loud and notice that none of them has an umlaut.
Describe three things you could not do last week, each starting with Ich konnte nicht ...
Describe two things you had to do yesterday with Ich musste ...

items: What happens to the umlaut of können, müssen and dürfen in the simple past?
Which two forms are identical, the ich form and which other one?
How do you say 'I wanted to' and 'I was supposed to'?

activities.title: Modalverben in der Vergangenheit
activities.intro: The modal verbs form their simple past by dropping the umlaut and adding -te: können becomes konnte, müssen becomes musste, dürfen becomes durfte, wollen becomes wollte, sollen becomes sollte and mögen becomes mochte. The ich form and the er form are identical and carry no extra ending. Unlike most verbs, these forms are completely normal in spoken German.

items.hr: Ich konnte gestern nicht kommen.
items.en: I could not come yesterday.
items.note: können -> konnte, the umlaut disappears.

items.hr: Wir mussten lange warten.
items.en: We had to wait a long time.
items.note: müssen -> musste, with ss because the u is short.

items.hr: Früher durfte ich nicht lange fernsehen.
items.en: In the past I was not allowed to watch television for long.
items.note: dürfen -> durfte.

items.hr: Sie wollte einen Kaffee bestellen.
items.en: She wanted to order a coffee.
items.note: wollen -> wollte and sollen -> sollte, both without any vowel change.

items.hr: Ich mochte das Essen nicht.
items.en: I did not like the food.
items.note: mögen -> mochte with ch, and it means liking, not wanting.

activities.title: Konnte, musste, wollte
activities.intro: Practise konnte, musste, wollte and durfte across all the persons.

questions.prompt: What is the Präteritum of 'müssen' in the ich form?
questions.explanation: The umlaut disappears and the short u is followed by ss, so the ich form is musste.

questions.prompt: Complete: Früher ___ ich kein Gemüse.
questions.explanation: mögen forms its simple past as mochte, while möchte is a polite present form.

questions.prompt: Write the simple past of the verb meaning 'to have to': Wir ___ zwei Stunden warten.
questions.explanation: The wir form adds -n to the base form of the simple past.

questions.prompt: Write the simple past of the verb meaning 'to be able to': Er ___ gestern nicht arbeiten, weil er krank war.
questions.explanation: The er form has no extra ending and no umlaut.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I could not sleep yesterday.'
questions.explanation: The modal verb is conjugated in second position and the infinitive closes the sentence.

activities.title: Warum warst du nicht da?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You explain to a colleague why you missed yesterday's meeting, reaching for konnte, musste and wollte.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Warum warst du gestern nicht im Kurs?
lines.en: Why were you not in class yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich konnte nicht kommen, weil ich krank war.
lines.en: I could not come because I was ill.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Musstest du zum Arzt?
lines.en: Did you have to go to the doctor?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich musste zwei Stunden warten.
lines.en: Yes, I had to wait for two hours.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wolltest du nicht anrufen?
lines.en: Did you not want to call?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich wollte anrufen, aber mein Handy war leer.
lines.en: I wanted to call, but my phone was dead.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und heute? Darfst du wieder arbeiten?
lines.en: And today? Are you allowed to work again?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gestern durfte ich nicht, heute geht es wieder.
lines.en: Yesterday I was not allowed to, today it is fine again.

days.title: Das Präteritum der regelmäßigen Verben
days.objective: You can form and recognise the simple past of regular verbs, including the extra e in stems ending in t or d.
days.paretoFocus: This is the tense of written German, so it is what you meet in every text, report or story you read, even though you will speak mostly in the perfect.
drills: Conjugate kaufen in the simple past through all six persons out loud.
Do the same with arbeiten and listen for the extra e before the ending.
Turn three perfect sentences you already know into the simple past, as if writing them down.

items: Which letters do you add to a regular stem to build the simple past?
Why does arbeiten become arbeitete and not arbeitte?
Which two persons share exactly the same form?

activities.title: Das Präteritum der regelmäßigen Verben
activities.intro: Regular verbs build the simple past from the stem plus -te plus the personal ending: ich kaufte, du kauftest, er kaufte, wir kauften, ihr kauftet, sie kauften. The ich form and the er form are identical. If the stem ends in t or d, an extra e appears so that the word stays pronounceable: arbeiten becomes arbeitete.

items.hr: Ich kaufte gestern eine Fahrkarte.
items.en: I bought a ticket yesterday.
items.note: kaufen -> kaufte, stem plus te.

items.hr: Wir wohnten zehn Jahre in Hamburg.
items.en: We lived in Hamburg for ten years.
items.note: The wir form adds -n: wohnten.

items.hr: Sie arbeitete bei einer Firma am Hafen.
items.en: She worked at a company at the harbour.
items.note: Stems in t take an extra e: arbeit + e + te.

items.hr: Der Film dauerte zwei Stunden.
items.en: The film lasted two hours.
items.note: dauern -> dauerte, the er form has no further ending.

items.hr: Er antwortete nicht auf meine Frage.
items.en: He did not answer my question.
items.note: antworten -> antwortete, again with the extra e.

activities.title: Regelmäßig in der Vergangenheit
activities.intro: Practise the te forms, watching for stems that need the extra e.

questions.prompt: What is the Präteritum of 'arbeiten' in the er form?
questions.explanation: The stem ends in t, so an e is inserted before the -te ending.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence:
questions.explanation: The wir form of the simple past is stem plus te plus n, and wohntet belongs to ihr.

questions.prompt: Write the simple past of the verb meaning 'to buy': Ich ___ gestern zwei Brötchen.
questions.explanation: Regular verbs take stem plus te, and the ich form adds nothing else.

questions.prompt: Write the simple past of the verb meaning 'to last': Der Kurs ___ drei Monate.
questions.explanation: The subject der Kurs takes the er form, which is identical to the ich form.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'He did not answer my question.'
questions.explanation: The stem ends in t, so the simple past inserts an extra e, and the er form takes no further ending.

activities.title: Ein Text über eine Familie
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You read a written text aloud to a friend, which is exactly the situation where the simple past belongs.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was steht in dem Text?
lines.en: What does the text say?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Familie Müller wohnte zwanzig Jahre in Hamburg.
lines.en: The Müller family lived in Hamburg for twenty years.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was machte der Vater?
lines.en: And what did the father do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er arbeitete bei einer Firma am Hafen.
lines.en: He worked at a company at the harbour.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie lange dauerte das?
lines.en: How long did that last?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Es dauerte bis zum Jahr 2010.
lines.en: It lasted until the year 2010.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was passierte dann?
lines.en: What happened then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Familie kaufte ein Haus und wohnte dann in Bremen.
lines.en: The family bought a house and then lived in Bremen.

days.title: Das Präteritum der unregelmäßigen Verben
days.objective: You can recognise and use the simple past of the most frequent irregular verbs, which change their stem vowel and take no -te.
days.paretoFocus: A small group of irregular verbs covers most of what happens in any story, so learning their past stems lets you read narratives without stopping.
drills: Say the pairs gehen and ging, kommen and kam, fahren and fuhr, sehen and sah, finden and fand out loud five times.
Put each of those five past forms into a short sentence about yesterday.
Change three of your sentences into the wir form and add the ending -en.

items: Do irregular verbs add -te in the simple past?
Which ending does the ich form take?
What are the past forms of gehen, kommen and fahren?

activities.title: Starke Verben im Präteritum
activities.intro: Irregular verbs, also called strong verbs, do not add -te. Instead they change the vowel of the stem, and the ich form and the er form carry no ending at all: ich ging, er ging. The wir form and the sie form add -en, du adds -st and ihr adds -t. There is no shortcut here, so the past stems are learned verb by verb.

items.hr: Ich ging jeden Tag zu Fuß zur Schule.
items.en: I walked to school every day.
items.note: gehen -> ging, with no ending in the ich and er forms.

items.hr: Er kam um sieben Uhr nach Hause.
items.en: He came home at seven o'clock.
items.note: kommen -> kam.

items.hr: Wir fuhren mit dem Zug nach München.
items.en: We travelled to Munich by train.
items.note: fahren -> fuhr, and the wir form adds -en.

items.hr: Sie sah den Film und fand ihn gut.
items.en: She saw the film and thought it was good.
items.note: sehen -> sah and finden -> fand.

items.hr: Ich saß am Fenster und schrieb einen Brief.
items.en: I sat at the window and wrote a letter.
items.note: sitzen -> saß and schreiben -> schrieb, and ß follows the long vowel in saß.

activities.title: Unregelmäßige Formen
activities.intro: Practise the past stems of the most common strong verbs.

questions.prompt: What is the Präteritum of 'gehen' in the ich form?
questions.explanation: Strong verbs change the stem vowel and take no -te, and gegangen is the participle, not the simple past.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is in the Präteritum and correct?
questions.explanation: The past stem is fuhr and the wir form simply adds -en, never -te.

questions.prompt: Write the simple past of the verb meaning 'to come': Er ___ gestern spät nach Hause.
questions.explanation: The stem vowel changes from o to a and the er form has no ending.

questions.prompt: Write the simple past of the verb meaning 'to sit': Ich ___ am Fenster und las ein Buch.
questions.explanation: The past stem has a long vowel, which is why the word is spelled with ß and not with ss.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We travelled to Munich by train.'
questions.explanation: The means of transport takes mit plus dative, and the past form fuhren stays in second position.

activities.title: Was passierte im Buch?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You retell the plot of a book, so every past form is a strong verb in the simple past.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie fing die Geschichte an?
lines.en: How did the story begin?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ein Mann ging spät nach Hause.
lines.en: A man was walking home late.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und dann?
lines.en: And then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er sah ein Licht und fand einen Schlüssel.
lines.en: He saw a light and found a key.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was machte er mit dem Schlüssel?
lines.en: What did he do with the key?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er nahm ihn mit und schrieb einen Brief.
lines.en: He took it with him and wrote a letter.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wohin fuhr er danach?
lines.en: Where did he travel afterwards?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er fuhr mit dem Zug nach Berlin.
lines.en: He travelled to Berlin by train.

days.title: Perfekt oder Präteritum, wann man was benutzt
days.objective: You can choose the past tense a German speaker would actually use, the perfect when speaking and the simple past when writing, with sein, haben and the modal verbs as fixed exceptions.
days.paretoFocus: Choosing the wrong past tense never blocks understanding but marks you instantly as a learner, and the rule behind the choice is short enough to master in one lesson.
drills: Tell yourself three things about yesterday out loud, using the perfect for every full verb.
In the same three sentences, replace any form of sein, haben or a modal verb with the simple past.
Say the pairs ich war, ich hatte, ich musste, ich konnte until they come without thinking.

items: Which past tense dominates in spoken German?
Which three groups of verbs keep the simple past even in speech?
Which auxiliary do sitzen, stehen and liegen take in the perfect?

activities.title: Perfekt oder Präteritum
activities.intro: In spoken German the perfect does almost all of the work for the past, while the simple past belongs to written texts and to narration. There is one important exception in both directions: sein, haben and the modals use the simple past even in conversation, because ich war and ich hatte sound far more natural than the perfect forms.
