Note also that sitzen, stehen and liegen take haben in the perfect in standard German.

items.hr: Gestern habe ich meine Freundin getroffen.
items.en: Yesterday I met my friend.
items.note: In conversation a full verb like treffen goes into the perfect.

items.hr: Ich war gestern sehr müde.
items.en: I was very tired yesterday.
items.note: sein keeps the simple past in speech: war, not bin gewesen.

items.hr: Wir hatten keine Zeit.
items.en: We had no time.
items.note: haben behaves the same way: hatten in speech and in writing.

items.hr: Ich musste gestern lange arbeiten.
items.en: I had to work late yesterday.
items.note: Modal verbs also stay in the simple past when you speak.

items.hr: Ich habe zwei Stunden im Wartezimmer gesessen.
items.en: I sat in the waiting room for two hours.
items.note: Standard German uses haben here: hat gesessen, hat gestanden, hat gelegen.

activities.title: Die richtige Vergangenheit wählen
activities.intro: Practise picking the past form that sounds natural in each situation.

questions.prompt: You are telling a friend about yesterday. Which sentence sounds most natural?
options: Gestern bin ich ins Kino gegangen.
Gestern ich bin ins Kino gegangen.
Gestern habe ich ins Kino gegangen.
questions.answer: Gestern bin ich ins Kino gegangen.
questions.explanation: Speech prefers the perfect for full verbs, and gehen takes sein as its auxiliary.

questions.prompt: Which form is normal in spoken German?
questions.explanation: sein is one of the verbs that keeps the simple past even in conversation.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct auxiliary: Ich ___ gestern lange im Wartezimmer gesessen.
questions.explanation: In standard German sitzen, stehen and liegen build the perfect with haben, not with sein.

questions.prompt: Use the natural spoken past of the verb meaning 'to have': Wir ___ leider keine Zeit.
questions.explanation: The perfect of haben sounds heavy in speech, so the simple past is used instead.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Yesterday I met my friend in the city.'
questions.explanation: Gestern fills position one, the auxiliary comes second, and the participle closes the sentence.

activities.title: Wie war dein Wochenende?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You chat about your weekend, using the perfect for the events but war, hatte and wollte where they sound natural.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie war dein Wochenende?
lines.en: How was your weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Es war schön, ich hatte endlich Zeit.
lines.en: It was nice, I finally had time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was hast du gemacht?
lines.en: What did you do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe Freunde getroffen und wir haben zusammen gekocht.
lines.en: I met friends and we cooked together.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Musstest du gar nicht arbeiten?
lines.en: Did you not have to work at all?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich musste nur am Freitag lange bleiben.
lines.en: No, I only had to stay late on Friday.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und am Sonntag?
lines.en: And on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Sonntag habe ich lange im Garten gesessen.
lines.en: On Sunday I sat in the garden for a long time.

days.title: Eine Geschichte in der Vergangenheit erzählen
days.objective: You can tell a connected story about the past, using time connectors such as zuerst, dann, danach, plötzlich and am Ende in the right word order.
days.paretoFocus: Anybody who asks how your day went expects a short story, and connectors are what turn a list of facts into something a listener can follow.
drills: Tell the story of your last journey in five sentences, opening each one with a different time connector.
Check every one of those sentences: the verb must be the second element, right after the connector.
Retell the same story once more and add one sentence with deshalb and one with weil.

items: What comes directly after a connector like dann or plötzlich?
Name five connectors that structure a story in order.
Which auxiliary do fahren and ankommen take in the perfect?

activities.title: Eine Geschichte erzählen
activities.intro: A story needs more than past forms, it needs signposts. zuerst, dann, danach, später, plötzlich and am Ende tell the listener where they are in the sequence. Each of these words takes the first position of the sentence, so the conjugated verb has to follow immediately and the subject comes after it, exactly as with deshalb.

items.hr: Zuerst bin ich zum Bahnhof gefahren.
items.en: First I went to the station.
items.note: zuerst is position one, so bin comes second and ich follows.

items.hr: Dann habe ich eine Fahrkarte gekauft.
items.en: Then I bought a ticket.
items.note: The participle stays at the end of the sentence.

items.hr: Plötzlich hat mein Handy geklingelt.
items.en: Suddenly my phone rang.
items.note: plötzlich marks a surprise and still forces verb before subject.

items.hr: Danach musste ich eine Stunde warten, deshalb habe ich einen Kaffee getrunken.
items.en: After that I had to wait for an hour, so I drank a coffee.
items.note: The modal verb stays in the simple past while the full verb goes into the perfect.

items.hr: Am Ende bin ich um zehn Uhr zu Hause angekommen.
items.en: In the end I arrived home at ten o'clock.
items.note: ankommen takes sein in the perfect: ist angekommen.

activities.title: Verbindungswörter im Text
activities.intro: Practise the connectors and the word order they force.

questions.prompt: Which word order is correct after a time word at the start of the sentence?
options: Dann ich habe einen Kaffee getrunken.
Dann habe ich einen Kaffee getrunken.
Dann habe einen Kaffee ich getrunken.
Dann getrunken habe ich einen Kaffee.
questions.answer: Dann habe ich einen Kaffee getrunken.
questions.explanation: The connector occupies position one, so the auxiliary is second and the subject comes right after it.

questions.prompt: Which connector introduces a surprise in a story?
questions.explanation: zuerst and danach order events in time and deshalb gives a consequence, while this word signals that something unexpected happens.

questions.prompt: Begin the story with a word meaning 'first of all': ___ bin ich aufgestanden, dann habe ich gefrühstückt.
questions.explanation: The connector fills position one, which is why the auxiliary bin stands before the subject.

questions.prompt: Which auxiliary does ankommen take? Ich ___ um zehn Uhr zu Hause angekommen.
questions.explanation: Verbs of movement to a place build the perfect with sein.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Then I bought a ticket at the station.'
questions.explanation: After the connector come the auxiliary and the subject, and the participle closes the sentence.

activities.title: Erzähl mal!
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks what happened yesterday and you answer with a connected story.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Erzähl mal, was ist gestern passiert?
lines.en: Tell me, what happened yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Zuerst bin ich zum Bahnhof gefahren.
lines.en: First I went to the station.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und dann?
lines.en: And then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann habe ich eine Fahrkarte gekauft und auf den Zug gewartet.
lines.en: Then I bought a ticket and waited for the train.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: War der Zug pünktlich?
lines.en: Was the train on time?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, plötzlich kam eine Durchsage, deshalb musste ich eine Stunde warten.
lines.en: No, suddenly there was an announcement, so I had to wait for an hour.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Oh nein. Was hast du gemacht?
lines.en: Oh no. What did you do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe einen Kaffee getrunken, und am Ende bin ich um zehn Uhr angekommen.
lines.en: I drank a coffee, and in the end I arrived at ten o'clock.

days.title: Der Komparativ
days.objective: You can form the comparative of any German adjective and use it to compare two things.
days.paretoFocus: Choosing, shopping and giving an opinion all run on comparatives, so one small ending unlocks hundreds of everyday sentences.
drills: Write six comparatives from adjectives you already know, and mark which ones added an Umlaut.
Compare three pairs of things in your home in full sentences, using 'als' each time.
Say out loud what you would rather drink, eat and watch tonight, using the comparative of 'gern'.

items: Which three very common adjectives have a completely irregular comparative?
Which kind of adjective adds an Umlaut in the comparative, and which never does?
Which little word introduces the second half of a comparison?

activities.title: Größer, besser, billiger
activities.intro: German builds the comparative by adding -er to the adjective, with no extra word in front of it. Most short one-syllable adjectives also take an Umlaut on a, o or u, while longer adjectives never do. A handful of everyday adjectives are irregular and simply have to be learned.

items.hr: Mein Bruder ist älter als ich.
items.en: My brother is older than I am.
items.note: alt -> älter, a short one-syllable adjective takes an Umlaut

items.hr: Das Fahrrad ist billiger als das Auto.
items.en: The bike is cheaper than the car.
items.note: billig -> billiger, adjectives of two syllables never add an Umlaut

items.hr: Heute ist das Wetter besser.
items.en: The weather is better today.
items.note: gut -> besser, completely irregular

items.hr: Ich fahre lieber mit dem Rad.
items.en: I would rather go by bike.
items.note: gern -> lieber, used for preferences

items.hr: Mein Koffer ist größer als deine Tasche.
items.en: My suitcase is bigger than your bag.
items.note: groß -> größer, the ß stays because the vowel is long

activities.title: Vergleiche üben
activities.intro: Practise building comparatives, including the irregular ones.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly compares the two cities?
options: Berlin ist mehr groß als München.
Berlin ist am größten als München.
questions.explanation: The comparative is the adjective plus -er, with an Umlaut on the o. German never uses an extra word like 'mehr' to build a normal comparative.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct comparative form of 'gut'.
questions.explanation: 'Gut' is irregular: gut, besser, am besten. The form 'guter' is an adjective ending, not a comparative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the comparative of 'fast': Der Zug ist ___ als der Bus.
questions.explanation: A regular one-syllable adjective with e in the stem takes -er and no Umlaut.

questions.prompt: Complete with the comparative of 'old': Meine Schwester ist ___ als ich.
questions.explanation: Short adjectives with a, o or u usually add an Umlaut in the comparative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I would rather drink coffee.'
questions.explanation: Preference is expressed with 'lieber', the comparative of 'gern', placed after the verb.

activities.title: Im Elektrogeschäft
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are comparing two laptops with a salesperson, so use a comparative in every answer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag, suchen Sie einen Laptop?
lines.en: Hello, are you looking for a laptop?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, aber dieser hier ist teurer als der andere.
lines.en: Yes, but this one here is more expensive than the other one.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das stimmt, dafür ist er auch schneller.
lines.en: That is true, but in exchange it is also faster.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Und welcher Akku hält länger?
lines.en: And which battery lasts longer?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Der Akku vom teuren Modell ist deutlich besser.
lines.en: The battery of the expensive model is clearly better.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut, dann nehme ich lieber das teurere Modell.
lines.en: Fine, then I would rather take the more expensive model.

days.title: Der Superlativ
days.objective: You can say that something is the most or the best of all, both after a verb and in front of a noun.
days.paretoFocus: Recommendations, favourites and rankings are everyday conversation, and the superlative is what carries them.
drills: Name your favourite month, city and food in full sentences with a superlative.
Write the three-step chain (positive, comparative, superlative) for six adjectives you use often.
Describe three things in your flat as the biggest, the oldest and the most beautiful.

items: When do you say 'am ...sten' and when do you put the superlative in front of the noun?
Which letters at the end of an adjective force an extra e before the -sten?
What is the full chain for 'gut' and for 'viel'?

activities.title: Am besten, am schönsten
activities.intro: After a verb, the superlative is 'am' plus the adjective plus -sten, as in 'am schnellsten'. Before a noun it behaves like an adjective and takes an ending, as in 'der schnellste Zug'. Adjectives ending in t, d, s, ß or z add an extra e first, and 'groß' keeps its ß as 'am größten'.

items.hr: Dieser Berg ist am höchsten.
items.en: This mountain is the highest.
items.note: hoch -> höher -> am höchsten

items.hr: Meine Wohnung ist am kleinsten.
items.en: My flat is the smallest.
items.note: the normal pattern: am plus Adjektiv plus -sten

items.hr: Der Juli ist der heißeste Monat.
items.en: July is the hottest month.
items.note: adjectives in ß, t, d, s or z insert an e: heißeste

items.hr: Was schmeckt dir am besten?
items.en: What do you like the taste of most?
items.note: gut -> besser -> am besten

items.hr: Im Dezember sind die Tage am kürzesten.
items.en: In December the days are the shortest.
items.note: kurz -> kürzer -> am kürzesten, Umlaut plus extra e

activities.title: Der Beste sein
activities.intro: Practise both superlative shapes, the am form and the attributive form with its ending.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct superlative of 'groß'?
questions.explanation: The o takes an Umlaut and the ß is kept because the vowel is long. The ß already contains the s sound, so no extra s is written.

questions.prompt: Complete: Von allen Filmen gefällt mir dieser ___.
questions.explanation: After a verb with no following noun, German uses the 'am ...sten' form, and 'gut' is irregular.

questions.prompt: Complete with the superlative of 'warm' in front of the noun: Der August ist ___ Monat des Jahres.
questions.explanation: In front of a noun the superlative takes the definite article and a normal adjective ending, here -e for the masculine nominative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the superlative of 'fast' after the verb: Wer von euch läuft ___?
questions.explanation: With no noun following, the 'am ...sten' form is used.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'This shop is the cheapest.'
questions.explanation: The superlative stands after 'ist' with no noun after it, so the 'am ...sten' form is correct.

activities.title: Der beste Kaffee der Stadt
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks for recommendations, so answer with superlatives.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo gibt es hier den besten Kaffee?
lines.en: Where is the best coffee around here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das kleine Café an der Ecke ist am besten.
lines.en: The small cafe on the corner is the best.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wo ist es am günstigsten?
lines.en: And where is it cheapest?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Im Supermarkt natürlich, aber dort schmeckt er am schlechtesten.
lines.en: In the supermarket of course, but there it tastes the worst.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wann ist das Café am vollsten?
lines.en: When is the cafe at its busiest?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Samstagmorgen, da sind die Schlangen am längsten.
lines.en: On Saturday morning, that is when the queues are the longest.

days.title: Vergleiche mit wie und als
days.objective: You can choose correctly between 'so ... wie' for things that are equal and a comparative plus 'als' for things that are not.
days.paretoFocus: Mixing up 'wie' and 'als' is the single most noticeable comparison mistake learners make, and fixing it makes you sound much more accurate.
drills: Write four sentences saying two things are equal, using 'so ... wie'.
Turn each of those four sentences into an unequal comparison with 'als'.
Say three things about today compared with yesterday, using the negative pattern 'nicht so ... wie'.

items: Which word follows a comparative, and which word follows 'so'?
How do you say two things are exactly equal, with more emphasis?
How do you say something is less than something else without using a new word?

activities.title: So groß wie du
activities.intro: When two things are the same, German uses 'so' before the plain adjective and 'wie' after it. When they are different, it uses the comparative followed by 'als'. To say something is less, the easiest route is the negative 'nicht so ... wie'.

items.hr: Ich bin so groß wie mein Vater.
items.en: I am as tall as my father.
items.note: equality: so plus plain adjective plus wie

items.hr: Der Kaffee hier ist besser als zu Hause.
items.en: The coffee here is better than at home.
items.note: after a comparative always als, never wie

items.hr: Heute ist es nicht so kalt wie gestern.
items.en: Today it is not as cold as yesterday.
items.note: the simplest way to say 'less cold than'

items.hr: Sie spricht besser Deutsch als ich.
items.en: She speaks German better than I do.
items.note: the pronoun after als stays in the nominative

items.hr: Mein Handy ist genauso teuer wie deins.
items.en: My phone is just as expensive as yours.
items.note: genauso ... wie makes the equality emphatic

activities.title: Wie oder als?
activities.intro: Decide each time whether the comparison is equal or unequal.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap: Er ist genauso alt ___ ich.
questions.explanation: The adjective is in its plain form after 'genauso', which signals equality, so the second part starts with 'wie'.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is correct in standard German?
options: Sie ist so älter wie ich.
Sie ist mehr alt als ich.
questions.explanation: A comparative form is always followed by 'als'. Using 'wie' after a comparative is a common spoken error but is not correct written German.

questions.prompt: Complete the equal comparison: Der Film war nicht so lang ___ das Buch.
questions.explanation: The adjective stays plain after 'so', so the comparison closes with 'wie'.

questions.prompt: Complete the unequal comparison: Zug fahren ist bequemer ___ Auto fahren.
questions.explanation: The adjective carries the comparative ending -er, and that ending always calls for 'als'.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'My room is as big as yours.'
questions.explanation: Equality puts 'so' before the plain adjective and 'wie' after it.

activities.title: Zwei Wohnungen vergleichen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Flat-hunting with a friend, you weigh the two options, so mix 'so ... wie' and comparatives with 'als'.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ist die zweite Wohnung so groß wie die erste?
lines.en: Is the second flat as big as the first one?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, sie ist kleiner als die erste, aber viel heller.
lines.en: No, it is smaller than the first one, but much brighter.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Miete? Ist sie auch teurer?
lines.en: And the rent? Is it more expensive too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sie ist genauso teuer wie die andere, leider.
lines.en: It is just as expensive as the other one, unfortunately.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Liegt sie wenigstens näher am Zentrum?
lines.en: Is it at least closer to the centre?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, und der Weg zur Arbeit ist nicht so lang wie jetzt.
lines.en: Yes, and the way to work is not as long as it is now.

days.title: Adjektivendungen nach dem bestimmten Artikel
days.objective: You can put the correct ending on an adjective that stands after der, die or das in front of a noun.
days.paretoFocus: Until now your adjectives only came after 'sein'; this is the table that lets you put them in front of nouns, which is where most real German sentences need them.
drills: Write the four nominative forms with 'gut' for masculine, feminine, neuter and plural.
Take five things in the room and describe each one twice, once as subject and once as object.
Write three sentences with a preposition that takes the dative, and check that every adjective ends in -en.

items: In which five cells of the table does the adjective end in -e?
What is the ending in every dative and every plural?
Why does 'den' change the ending but 'die' for feminine does not?

activities.title: Der gute Wein
activities.intro: After der, die, das, dieser or welcher, the adjective only ever ends in -e or -en. It ends in -e in the nominative singular of all three genders and in the accusative feminine and neuter. Everywhere else, which means the accusative masculine, the whole dative and the whole plural, it ends in -en.

items.hr: Der neue Lehrer ist sehr nett.
items.en: The new teacher is very nice.
items.note: Nominativ maskulin -> -e

items.hr: Ich kaufe die frische Milch.
items.en: I am buying the fresh milk.
items.note: Akkusativ feminin -> -e

items.hr: Ich trinke den kalten Saft.
items.en: I am drinking the cold juice.
items.note: Akkusativ maskulin -> -en, the article already shows the change

items.hr: Wir wohnen in dem alten Haus.
items.en: We live in the old house.
items.note: Dativ -> immer -en

items.hr: Die kleinen Kinder spielen im Garten.
items.en: The small children are playing in the garden.
items.note: Plural -> immer -en, in allen Fällen

activities.title: Nur -e oder -en
activities.intro: Practise the two possible endings after a definite article.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ich lese ___ Buch.
questions.explanation: 'Das Buch' is neuter and here it is the accusative object, and the accusative neuter is one of the cells that keeps -e.

questions.prompt: Complete: Wir fahren mit ___ Zug.
questions.explanation: 'Mit' takes the dative, so the masculine article is 'dem' and every dative adjective ends in -en.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'rot' (red): Ich nehme die ___ Jacke.
questions.explanation: 'Die Jacke' is feminine and stands in the accusative, which keeps the ending -e.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'klein' (small): Ich spiele mit dem ___ Hund.
questions.explanation: The dative after 'mit' forces -en, no matter what the gender is.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The young woman speaks German.'
questions.explanation: The feminine subject stands in the nominative singular, where the ending is -e.

activities.title: Im Kleidergeschäft
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are trying on clothes and every item is named with a definite article plus an adjective.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gefällt Ihnen die blaue Hose?
lines.en: Do you like the blue trousers?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, aber ich möchte lieber den grauen Pullover probieren.
lines.en: Yes, but I would rather try on the grey pullover.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Der graue Pullover hängt dort hinten.
lines.en: The grey pullover is hanging over there at the back.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Und was kostet das weiße Hemd neben der Tür?
lines.en: And how much is the white shirt next to the door?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das weiße Hemd kostet dreißig Euro.
lines.en: The white shirt costs thirty euros.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut, dann nehme ich das Hemd mit den kleinen Knöpfen.
lines.en: Good, then I will take the shirt with the small buttons.

days.title: Adjektivendungen nach dem unbestimmten Artikel
days.objective: You can put the correct ending on an adjective that stands after ein, kein or a possessive.
days.paretoFocus: Almost everything you mention for the first time comes with 'ein', so this is the table you will reach for most often in real conversation.
drills: Write the same noun twice, once with 'der' and once with 'ein', and mark where the ending differs.
Describe five things you own using 'mein' plus an adjective.
Write three sentences with 'kein' plus an adjective in the accusative.

items: In exactly which three cells does the ending differ from the definite article table?
Why does the adjective have to carry the ending in those three cells?
Which other little words follow this same pattern besides 'ein'?

activities.title: Ein guter Wein
activities.intro: After ein, kein, mein, dein and the other possessives, the endings are the same as after der, die, das with only three exceptions. Those three are the masculine nominative, the neuter nominative and the neuter accusative, where the article 'ein' has no ending of its own. Because 'ein' carries no gender signal there, the signal moves onto the adjective: der gute Wein but ein guter Wein, das kalte Bier but ein kaltes Bier.

items.hr: Das ist ein guter Wein.
items.en: That is a good wine.
items.note: der gute Wein aber ein guter Wein, das -r wandert auf das Adjektiv

items.hr: Wir trinken ein kaltes Bier.
items.en: We are drinking a cold beer.
items.note: das kalte Bier aber ein kaltes Bier, das -s wandert auf das Adjektiv

items.hr: Ich habe eine neue Wohnung.
items.en: I have a new flat.
items.note: feminine is unchanged because 'eine' already shows the gender

items.hr: Ich suche einen billigen Tisch.
items.en: I am looking for a cheap table.
items.note: Akkusativ maskulin -> -en, genau wie nach 'den'

items.hr: Sie kommt mit einem netten Freund.
items.en: She is coming with a nice friend.
items.note: Dativ -> immer -en, ohne Ausnahme

activities.title: Ein oder der?
activities.intro: Practise the three cells where 'ein' behaves differently.

questions.prompt: Complete: Er hat ___ Auto gekauft.
questions.explanation: 'Das Auto' is neuter and 'ein' shows no gender here, so the adjective takes the -s that 'das' would have carried.

questions.prompt: Complete: Das ist ___ Film.
questions.explanation: Masculine nominative after 'ein' moves the -r of 'der' onto the adjective.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'gut' (good): Ich habe eine ___ Idee.
questions.explanation: 'Eine' already marks the feminine, so this cell keeps the same -e as after 'die'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'alt' (old): Wir wohnen in einem ___ Haus.
questions.explanation: The dative always ends in -en, so the indefinite article changes nothing here.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am looking for a cheap hotel.'
questions.explanation: 'Das Hotel' is neuter, and the neuter accusative after 'ein' is one of the three cells that take -es.

activities.title: Eine neue Wohnung suchen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You describe what you are looking for, so almost every noun comes with 'ein' plus an adjective.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was für eine Wohnung suchen Sie denn?
lines.en: What kind of flat are you looking for?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich suche eine ruhige Wohnung mit einem kleinen Balkon.
lines.en: I am looking for a quiet flat with a small balcony.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wir haben ein helles Zimmer im dritten Stock.
lines.en: We have a bright room on the third floor.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gibt es auch ein modernes Bad?
lines.en: Is there a modern bathroom as well?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, und die Küche hat einen neuen Herd.
lines.en: Yes, and the kitchen has a new cooker.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das klingt nach einem guten Angebot.
lines.en: That sounds like a good offer.

days.title: Adjektivendungen ohne Artikel
days.objective: You can put the right ending on an adjective when there is no article in front of the noun at all.
days.paretoFocus: Food, drink and shopping lists almost never take an article in German, so this pattern shows up every time you order or buy something.
drills: Write a shopping list of eight items, each with an adjective and no article.
Say what you eat and drink on a normal day, using no articles at all.
Write three sentences with 'mit' plus an uncountable noun and an adjective.

items: Where does the ending come from when no article is present?
Which two kinds of noun make this pattern come up most often?
What is the ending in the dative plural, and what is it in the dative feminine?

activities.title: Guter Wein, kaltes Wasser
activities.intro: Without an article, the adjective itself takes the ending the article would have carried: der becomes guter, das becomes kaltes, dem becomes gutem. This happens mostly with uncountable nouns and with plurals, because those are the nouns German most often uses bare. So 'der Wein' gives 'guter Wein' and 'das Wasser' gives 'kaltes Wasser'.

items.hr: Guter Wein ist nicht immer teuer.
items.en: Good wine is not always expensive.
items.note: der Wein -> guter Wein, das -r vom Artikel

items.hr: Ich trinke gern kaltes Wasser.
items.en: I like drinking cold water.
items.note: das Wasser -> kaltes Wasser, das -s vom Artikel

items.hr: Zum Frühstück gibt es frische Brötchen.
items.en: For breakfast there are fresh bread rolls.
items.note: Plural im Akkusativ -> -e

items.hr: Ich koche mit frischer Milch.
items.en: I cook with fresh milk.
items.note: Dativ feminin -> -er, wie in 'der Milch'

items.hr: Wir haben heißen Tee bestellt.
items.en: We ordered hot tea.
items.note: Akkusativ maskulin -> -en, wie in 'den Tee'

activities.title: Ohne Artikel einkaufen
activities.intro: Practise adjective endings on bare nouns, mostly food and drink.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ich esse gern ___ Brot.
questions.explanation: 'Das Brot' is neuter and stands in the accusative, so the adjective takes the -s that 'das' would have shown.

questions.prompt: Complete: Auf dem Markt gibt es ___ Obst.
questions.explanation: 'Das Obst' is neuter and follows 'es gibt' in the accusative, which gives the ending -es.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'stark' (strong): Am Morgen trinke ich ___ Kaffee.
questions.explanation: 'Der Kaffee' is masculine and stands in the accusative here, so the adjective takes the -n of 'den'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'kalt' (cold), as the first word of the sentence: ___ Milch schmeckt mir am besten.
questions.explanation: 'Die Milch' is feminine and is the subject, so the adjective takes the -e of 'die'.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Fresh bread tastes good.'
questions.explanation: With no article, the neuter nominative shows up as -es on the adjective.

activities.title: Auf dem Markt
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are buying food, and bare nouns with adjectives are everywhere.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Morgen, was darf es sein?
lines.en: Good morning, what can I get you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich hätte gern frische Tomaten und grünen Salat.
lines.en: I would like some fresh tomatoes and green salad.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wir haben heute auch sehr guten Käse.
lines.en: We also have very good cheese today.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann nehme ich zweihundert Gramm, bitte.
lines.en: Then I will take two hundred grams, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und darf ich Ihnen frisches Obst anbieten?
lines.en: And may I offer you some fresh fruit?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, und bitte noch kaltes Wasser für zu Hause.
lines.en: Yes, and also some cold water for home, please.

days.title: Menschen beschreiben, Aussehen
days.objective: You can describe what a person looks like, using adjectives in front of nouns rather than only after 'sein'.
days.paretoFocus: Describing someone so another person can pick them out of a crowd is a real everyday task, and it forces every adjective ending at once.
drills: Describe three people you know in three sentences each, always putting the adjective before the noun.
Write how you would describe yourself to someone meeting you at a station for the first time.
Take a photo you like and list five details of the people in it, each with an adjective plus a noun.

items: How do you say 'she has long dark hair' with no article in front of the adjectives?
What happens to the e of 'dunkel' when an ending is added?
Which ending do all adjectives take after 'mit den'?

activities.title: Wie sieht sie aus?
activities.intro: Physical description in German almost always puts the adjective in front of the noun, so all three ending tables come into play at once. Hair and eyes are plural and usually appear with no article, which gives the -e ending. Note that 'dunkel' and 'teuer' drop the e of the stem when an ending is added.

items.hr: Sie hat lange blonde Haare.
items.en: She has long blond hair.
items.note: Plural ohne Artikel -> beide Adjektive auf -e

items.hr: Mein Onkel ist ein großer schlanker Mann.
items.en: My uncle is a tall slim man.
items.note: nach 'ein' im Nominativ maskulin -> -er auf beiden Adjektiven

items.hr: Meine Schwester hat blaue Augen und eine kleine Nase.
items.en: My sister has blue eyes and a small nose.
items.note: die Nase ist feminin -> eine kleine Nase

items.hr: Der Mann mit der grauen Jacke ist mein Chef.
items.en: The man with the grey jacket is my boss.
items.note: mit + Dativ -> immer -en

items.hr: Wer ist die junge Frau mit der dunklen Brille?
items.en: Who is the young woman with the dark glasses?
items.note: dunkel -> dunkl-, das e fällt weg: dunklen

activities.title: Wen suchst du?
activities.intro: Practise describing appearance with adjectives in front of the noun.

questions.prompt: Complete: Er hat ___ Bart.
questions.explanation: 'Der Bart' is masculine and 'haben' takes the accusative, which gives 'einen' plus -en.

questions.prompt: Which sentence describes someone's build correctly?
questions.explanation: Feminine nominative after 'eine' keeps the ending -e, exactly as it does after 'die'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'dunkel' (dark), remembering that the stem loses its e: Sie hat ___ Haare.
questions.explanation: The plural with no article takes -e, and the stem shortens from dunkel to dunkl before an ending.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'grün' (green): Der Junge mit den ___ Schuhen ist mein Sohn.
questions.explanation: The dative plural always ends in -en, on the article and on the adjective alike.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'My father is a tall man.'
questions.explanation: 'Ein' carries no ending in the masculine nominative, so the -r moves onto the adjective.

activities.title: Am Bahnhof abholen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are describing a person on the phone so your friend can recognise them at the station.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie erkenne ich deine Kollegin am Bahnhof?
lines.en: How will I recognise your colleague at the station?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sie ist eine kleine Frau mit kurzen roten Haaren.
lines.en: She is a short woman with short red hair.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was trägt sie heute?
lines.en: And what is she wearing today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Einen dunklen Mantel und eine große schwarze Tasche.
lines.en: A dark coat and a big black bag.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Trägt sie eine Brille?
lines.en: Does she wear glasses?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, eine runde Brille, und sie hat freundliche blaue Augen.
lines.en: Yes, round glasses, and she has friendly blue eyes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut, ich glaube, ich finde sie.
lines.en: Good, I think I will find her.

days.title: Menschen beschreiben, Charakter
days.objective: You can describe someone's personality with adjectives placed in front of the noun, and say what kind of people you get on with.
days.paretoFocus: Talking about colleagues, friends and family is the backbone of small talk, and character adjectives are what make those descriptions say anything.
drills: List eight character adjectives with their opposites.
Describe three people you work or live with, using 'ein' plus an adjective plus a noun each time.
Write two sentences about the kind of people you like and two about the kind you find difficult.

items: How do you say 'he is a patient person' with the article 'ein'?
Which ending do adjectives take in a bare plural accusative, as in 'I like friendly people'?
What is the adjective form of 'gut' inside 'mit meinem besten Freund'?

activities.title: Was für ein Mensch ist er?
activities.intro: Character adjectives work exactly like the ones you have just learned, but they are worth practising separately because the nouns are different: der Mensch, der Kollege, die Nachbarin. Remember that 'der Mensch' is masculine, so it takes 'ein netter Mensch'. Bare plurals such as 'freundliche Menschen' take -e in the nominative and accusative.

items.hr: Meine Kollegin ist eine sehr geduldige Frau.
items.en: My colleague is a very patient woman.
items.note: feminin nach 'eine' -> -e

items.hr: Ich mag freundliche und ehrliche Menschen.
items.en: I like friendly and honest people.
items.note: Plural ohne Artikel im Akkusativ -> -e

items.hr: Unser neuer Chef ist ziemlich streng.
items.en: Our new boss is quite strict.
items.note: 'unser' verhält sich wie 'ein' -> neuer

items.hr: Mit meinem besten Freund kann ich über alles reden.
items.en: I can talk about anything with my best friend.
items.note: gut -> best-, im Dativ -> besten

items.hr: Sie ist ein sehr offener Mensch.
items.en: She is a very open person.
items.note: der Mensch ist maskulin, auch bei einer Frau -> offener

activities.title: Charakter beschreiben
activities.intro: Practise character adjectives with the right endings.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ich arbeite mit ___ Kollegen.
questions.explanation: This is a bare dative plural after 'mit', and the dative plural always ends in -en.

questions.prompt: Complete: Er ist ___ Mensch.
questions.explanation: 'Der Mensch' is masculine and this is the nominative after 'sein', so the adjective carries the -r.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'ehrlich' (honest): Ich suche einen ___ Partner.
questions.explanation: 'Suchen' takes the accusative, and the masculine accusative ends in -en after 'einen'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'lustig' (funny): Wir haben eine ___ Nachbarin.
questions.explanation: Feminine accusative keeps the ending -e, both after 'die' and after 'eine'.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I like calm people.'
questions.explanation: A bare plural in the accusative takes the -e that 'die' would have carried.

activities.title: Über den neuen Kollegen sprechen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague asks about the new person on the team, so describe character with adjectives in front of nouns.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie ist eigentlich der neue Kollege?
lines.en: What is the new colleague actually like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er ist ein sehr ruhiger Mensch, aber sehr hilfsbereit.
lines.en: He is a very quiet person, but very helpful.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Arbeitet er gern im Team?
lines.en: Does he like working in a team?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, er mag offene Gespräche und ehrliche Kritik.
lines.en: Yes, he likes open conversations and honest criticism.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das klingt besser als bei unserem alten Chef.
lines.en: That sounds better than with our old boss.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Genau, mit einem geduldigen Kollegen läuft alles leichter.
lines.en: Exactly, everything runs more easily with a patient colleague.

days.title: Ordnungszahlen und das Datum
days.objective: You can form German ordinal numbers and use them to say and write any date correctly.
days.paretoFocus: Appointments, birthdays and forms all need the date, and German writes and says it with an ordinal that carries an adjective ending.
drills: Write the ordinals from first to twelfth and mark the three irregular ones.
Write the birthdays of five people you know, using 'am' plus the ordinal.
Write today's date in the German written form and then say the full sentence out loud.

items: What does the full stop after a number mean in German?
Where does the ending switch from -t- to -st-, and which three ordinals are irregular?
Which ending does the ordinal take after 'am', and why?

activities.title: Der Wievielte ist heute?
activities.intro: Ordinals up to nineteen add -t- to the number and from twenty onwards they add -st-, so vier gives vierte and zwanzig gives zwanzigste. Three are irregular: erste, dritte and siebte, and achte has only one t. In writing, a full stop after the figure marks the ordinal, so 3. Mai is read as 'der dritte Mai', and because the ordinal is an adjective it takes an adjective ending, which is why 'am' gives 'am dritten Mai'.

items.hr: Heute ist der dritte Mai.
items.en: Today is the third of May.
items.note: geschrieben: der 3. Mai, der Punkt markiert die Ordnungszahl

items.hr: Mein Kurs beginnt am ersten September.
items.en: My course starts on the first of September.
items.note: am + Dativ -> ersten, wie jedes Adjektiv

items.hr: Ich habe am zwanzigsten August Geburtstag.
items.en: My birthday is on the twentieth of August.
items.note: ab 20. kommt -st-: zwanzigste, zwanzigsten

items.hr: Sie wohnt im siebten Stock.
items.en: She lives on the seventh floor.
items.note: sieben -> siebte, das n fällt weg

items.hr: Der achte Dezember ist ein Montag.
items.en: The eighth of December is a Monday.
items.note: acht -> achte, nur ein t

activities.title: Termine und Daten
activities.intro: Practise forming ordinals and putting them into dates.

questions.prompt: How do you say the written date 1. Juni?
questions.explanation: The ordinal of 'eins' is irregular and the nominative masculine takes -e after 'der'.

questions.prompt: Complete: Wir fahren ___ dritten April in den Urlaub.
questions.explanation: A specific day takes 'am', which is 'an dem' and therefore the dative, which is why the ordinal ends in -en.

questions.prompt: Write out the ordinal for the figure 3 in this sentence: Heute ist der ___ Oktober.
questions.explanation: This ordinal is irregular, and after 'der' in the nominative it takes -e.

questions.prompt: Write out the ordinal for the figure 20 in this sentence: Der Kurs endet am ___ Juli.
questions.explanation: From twenty upwards the ordinal is built with -st-, and 'am' is dative, so the ending is -en.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'My birthday is on the seventh of March.'
questions.explanation: The ordinal loses the n of 'sieben' and then takes the dative ending -en after 'am'.

activities.title: Einen Termin vereinbaren
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are arranging an appointment by phone, so every date is spoken with an ordinal.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag, wann möchten Sie den Termin?
lines.en: Hello, when would you like the appointment?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Geht es am zweiten Februar am Vormittag?
lines.en: Would the second of February in the morning work?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Leider nicht, aber der fünfte Februar ist frei.
lines.en: Unfortunately not, but the fifth of February is free.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Der fünfte passt mir gut. Um wie viel Uhr?
lines.en: The fifth suits me well. At what time?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Um halb elf. Und Ihr Geburtsdatum, bitte?
lines.en: At half past ten. And your date of birth, please?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich bin am einunddreißigsten Januar geboren.
lines.en: I was born on the thirty first of January.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Danke, dann sehen wir uns am fünften Februar.
lines.en: Thank you, then we will see each other on the fifth of February.

days.title: Gefühle ausdrücken
days.objective: You can say how you feel and why, using the fixed expressions German prefers and adjectives in front of nouns.
days.paretoFocus: German expresses many feelings with 'haben' plus a noun rather than with 'sein' plus an adjective, so learning the noun patterns is what makes you sound natural.
drills: Write five sentences with 'ich habe' plus a feeling noun and an adjective in front of it.
Write three sentences naming a feeling and its cause, joined with 'weil'.
Note down how you felt at three moments last week, using the perfect or the Präteritum.

items: Which case follows 'sich freuen über', and which one follows 'Angst haben vor'?
How do you say 'I am in a good mood' with no article at all?
Which ending does the adjective take in 'nach einer ___ Woche'?

activities.title: Wie geht es dir wirklich?
activities.intro: Many German feelings are built with 'haben' plus a noun, such as Angst haben or Lust haben, and those nouns usually appear with no article, which is where the bare adjective endings come back. Two verbs are worth memorising with their preposition: 'sich freuen über' takes the accusative and 'Angst haben vor' takes the dative. Add 'weil' to give the reason.

items.hr: Ich hatte einen schlechten Tag.
items.en: I had a bad day.
items.note: Akkusativ maskulin nach 'einen' -> -en

items.hr: Ich freue mich über die gute Nachricht.
items.en: I am happy about the good news.
items.note: sich freuen über + Akkusativ

items.hr: Ich bin nervös, weil ich morgen ein wichtiges Gespräch habe.
items.en: I am nervous because I have an important conversation tomorrow.
items.note: das Gespräch ist neutral -> ein wichtiges Gespräch

items.hr: Wir haben große Angst vor dem Fliegen.
items.en: We are very afraid of flying.
items.note: Angst haben vor + Dativ, 'Angst' ohne Artikel -> große

items.hr: Nach dem langen Tag bin ich einfach müde.
items.en: After the long day I am simply tired.
items.note: nach + Dativ -> immer -en

activities.title: Über Gefühle sprechen
activities.intro: Practise the fixed feeling expressions together with adjective endings.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ich habe heute ___ Laune.
questions.explanation: 'Die Laune' is feminine and stands in the accusative with no article, so the adjective takes the -e of 'die'.

questions.prompt: Complete: Er ärgert sich über ___ Wetter.
questions.explanation: 'Sich ärgern über' takes the accusative, and the neuter accusative after 'das' keeps the ending -e.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'groß' (big): Ich habe ___ Lust auf einen Spaziergang.
questions.explanation: 'Die Lust' is feminine and appears here with no article in the accusative, which gives -e.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'lang' (long): Nach einer ___ Woche schlafe ich gern aus.
questions.explanation: 'Nach' takes the dative, and every dative adjective ends in -en.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'She is afraid of the big dog.'
questions.explanation: Fear is expressed with 'Angst haben vor', and 'vor' takes the dative here, so the adjective ends in -en.

activities.title: Ein schwieriger Tag
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend notices you are down, so talk about your feelings and give reasons.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Du siehst müde aus. Ist alles in Ordnung?
lines.en: You look tired. Is everything all right?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich hatte einen sehr stressigen Tag im Büro.
lines.en: I had a very stressful day at the office.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was ist denn passiert?
lines.en: What happened then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe Angst vor der neuen Prüfung nächste Woche.
lines.en: I am afraid of the new exam next week.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das verstehe ich, aber du hast gut gelernt.
lines.en: I understand that, but you have studied well.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Stimmt, und ich freue mich über deine netten Worte.
lines.en: True, and I am happy about your kind words.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Komm, wir trinken einen heißen Tee zusammen.
lines.en: Come on, let us have a hot tea together.

days.title: Eine Wohnung suchen, die Anzeige lesen
days.objective: Read a German rental advert and understand what the money and the abbreviations actually mean.
days.paretoFocus: Almost every flat search in Germany starts with the same handful of words, and misreading Kaltmiete as the real cost is the single most expensive beginner mistake.
drills: Write your own three line advert for the flat you would like to rent, using at least one adjective before each noun.
Say out loud what your total monthly cost would be if the cold rent is 850 Euro and the extra costs are 180 Euro.
List five things you would want an advert to tell you, each as a full question.

items: What is the difference between Kaltmiete and Warmmiete?
What is the Kaution for, and do you get it back?
Which floor is the Erdgeschoss?

activities.title: Die Wohnungsanzeige
activities.intro: Rental adverts are short and full of fixed words. The rent you see first is usually the Kaltmiete, the rent without heating, water and rubbish collection. Add the Nebenkosten and you get the Warmmiete, which is what actually leaves your account every month.

items.hr: Wir suchen eine helle Wohnung mit einem großen Balkon.
items.en: We are looking for a bright flat with a big balcony.
items.note: eine helle Wohnung (accusative feminine), mit einem großen Balkon (dative masculine).

items.hr: Die Kaltmiete beträgt 850 Euro, die Nebenkosten kosten 180 Euro extra.
items.en: The cold rent is 850 euros, the extra costs are another 180 euros.
items.note: die Kaltmiete beträgt ... is the normal wording for a sum of money.

items.hr: Die Warmmiete ist die Kaltmiete plus die Nebenkosten, also 1030 Euro.
items.en: The warm rent is the cold rent plus the extra costs, so 1030 euros.
items.note: Always ask which of the two figures an advert is showing.

items.hr: Die Kaution beträgt drei Kaltmieten, und man bekommt sie beim Auszug zurück.
items.en: The deposit is three months of cold rent, and you get it back when you move out.
items.note: die Kaution, the deposit, is legally limited to three cold rents.

items.hr: Die möblierte Wohnung im Erdgeschoss hat 65 Quadratmeter und liegt in einem alten Altbau.
items.en: The furnished ground floor flat has 65 square metres and is in an old period building.
items.note: der Altbau is a building from before roughly 1950, der Neubau a modern one.

activities.title: Zahlen und Wörter in der Anzeige
activities.intro: Practise the money words from rental adverts and the adjective endings you need to describe a flat.

questions.prompt: Die Kaltmiete plus die Nebenkosten ergibt ...
questions.explanation: Warmmiete is the total monthly payment: the basic rent plus the running costs for heating, water and rubbish.

questions.prompt: Wir suchen eine ___ Wohnung mit einem ___ Balkon.
questions.explanation: After eine in the accusative the adjective takes -e; after einem in the dative masculine it takes -en.

questions.prompt: Vor dem Einzug zahlt man eine Sicherheit an den Vermieter, meistens drei Kaltmieten. Diese Summe heißt die ___.
questions.explanation: die Kaution is the security deposit. The landlord keeps it during the tenancy and returns it after you move out if there is no damage.

questions.prompt: Die Wohnung liegt nicht im ersten Stock, sondern ganz unten auf Straßenniveau, also im ___.
questions.explanation: das Erdgeschoss is the ground floor. The floor above it is called der erste Stock, not the second.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We are looking for a bright flat with a big balcony.'
questions.explanation: Verb in second position, then the accusative object, then the prepositional phrase with mit, which always takes the dative.

activities.title: Wir lesen eine Anzeige
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend shows you a rental advert and you work out what it really costs, using the advert vocabulary and adjectives before nouns.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schau mal, hier ist eine schöne Wohnung: drei Zimmer, 78 Quadratmeter, Altbau.
lines.en: Look, here is a nice flat: three rooms, 78 square metres, period building.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das klingt gut. Was kostet sie denn? Ist das die Kaltmiete oder die Warmmiete?
lines.en: That sounds good. What does it cost? Is that the cold rent or the warm rent?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hier stehen 890 Euro Kaltmiete und 190 Euro Nebenkosten.
lines.en: It says 890 euros cold rent and 190 euros extra costs.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann zahlen wir jeden Monat 1080 Euro. Das ist teurer als unsere alte Wohnung.
lines.en: Then we pay 1080 euros every month. That is more expensive than our old flat.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, aber sie hat einen großen Balkon und liegt im Erdgeschoss.
lines.en: Yes, but it has a big balcony and it is on the ground floor.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Und die Kaution? Drei Kaltmieten sind fast 2700 Euro auf einmal.
lines.en: And the deposit? Three cold rents is almost 2700 euros all at once.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das stimmt. Aber das Geld bekommen wir beim Auszug wieder zurück.
lines.en: That is true. But we get that money back when we move out.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut, dann schreibe ich heute Abend eine kurze Nachricht und frage nach einem Termin.
lines.en: Good, then I will write a short message this evening and ask for an appointment.

days.title: Die Wohnungsbesichtigung
days.objective: Ask the right questions at a flat viewing, using polite indirect questions with wann and ob.
days.paretoFocus: A viewing lasts fifteen minutes and there are often ten other people there, so the questions you ask in that quarter of an hour decide whether you get the flat and whether you regret it.
drills: Turn five direct questions about a flat into indirect ones beginning with Ich möchte wissen, ...
Describe two rooms of your own home using an adjective before each noun.
Write the three questions you would ask first at a viewing and say why they matter.

items: Where does the verb go in a clause after ob?
How do you ask when the flat becomes available, politely?
Which costs should you always ask about at a viewing?

activities.title: Fragen bei der Besichtigung
activities.intro: At a viewing you want to sound polite but get real information. German does this with indirect questions: you start with Können Sie mir sagen or Ich möchte wissen, and the question word or ob introduces a clause with the verb at the very end.

items.hr: Können Sie mir sagen, wann die Wohnung frei wird?
items.en: Can you tell me when the flat becomes available?
items.note: wird goes to the end of the clause after wann.

items.hr: Ich möchte wissen, ob die Heizkosten in den Nebenkosten enthalten sind.
items.en: I would like to know whether the heating costs are included in the extra costs.
items.note: ob introduces a yes or no question inside a sentence.

items.hr: Wie hoch ist die Kaution, und wann bekomme ich sie zurück?
items.en: How high is the deposit, and when do I get it back?
items.note: wie hoch, not wie viel, is the normal question for a sum of money.

items.hr: Wer bezahlt kleine Reparaturen in der Wohnung, der Vermieter oder der Mieter?
items.en: Who pays for small repairs in the flat, the landlord or the tenant?
items.note: der Vermieter rents the flat out, der Mieter lives in it.

items.hr: Die Wohnung hat ein kleines Bad, aber eine große Küche mit einem neuen Herd.
items.en: The flat has a small bathroom but a big kitchen with a new cooker.
items.note: ein kleines Bad (neuter), eine große Küche (feminine), mit einem neuen Herd (dative).

activities.title: Höflich fragen
activities.intro: Practise indirect questions and the adjective endings you need to talk about rooms.

questions.prompt: Welcher Satz ist richtig?
options: Ich möchte wissen, wann die Wohnung frei wird.
Ich möchte wissen, wann wird die Wohnung frei.
Ich möchte wissen, wann frei die Wohnung wird.
Ich möchte wissen, wann wird frei die Wohnung.
questions.answer: Ich möchte wissen, wann die Wohnung frei wird.
questions.explanation: In an indirect question the conjugated verb moves to the end of the clause. Only the first sentence does that.

questions.prompt: Die Wohnung hat ein ___ Bad, aber eine ___ Küche.
questions.explanation: ein Bad is neuter, so the adjective takes -es in the accusative; eine Küche is feminine and takes -e.

questions.prompt: Der Termin, an dem man die Wohnung zum ersten Mal anschaut, heißt die ___.
questions.explanation: die Besichtigung is the viewing. The verb is besichtigen: Wir besichtigen die Wohnung am Freitag.

questions.prompt: Ich möchte wissen, ___ die Heizkosten in den Nebenkosten enthalten sind.
questions.explanation: A yes or no question inside a sentence is introduced by ob, never by wenn. wenn is only for time or conditions.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Can you tell me when the flat becomes available?'
questions.explanation: The main clause is a normal question with the verb first, and the indirect question after wann puts wird at the end.

activities.title: Bei der Besichtigung
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are viewing a flat and the landlord shows you around; ask about the deposit, the heating and the date with indirect questions.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag, kommen Sie herein. Das ist das Wohnzimmer mit den zwei großen Fenstern.
lines.en: Hello, do come in. This is the living room with the two big windows.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sehr schön und hell. Können Sie mir sagen, wann die Wohnung frei wird?
lines.en: Very nice and bright. Can you tell me when the flat becomes available?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Die jetzige Mieterin zieht am 30. September aus, ab Oktober ist die Wohnung frei.
lines.en: The current tenant moves out on 30 September, so the flat is free from October.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut. Ich möchte auch wissen, ob die Heizkosten in den Nebenkosten enthalten sind.
lines.en: Good. I would also like to know whether the heating costs are included in the extra costs.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, Heizung, Wasser und Müll sind dabei. Nur den Strom melden Sie selbst an.
lines.en: Yes, heating, water and rubbish are included. You only register the electricity yourself.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Und wie hoch ist die Kaution?
lines.en: And how high is the deposit?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Drei Kaltmieten, also 2400 Euro. Sie können in drei Raten zahlen.
lines.en: Three cold rents, so 2400 euros. You can pay in three instalments.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das ist gut zu wissen. Darf ich noch schnell das kleine Bad sehen?
lines.en: That is good to know. May I quickly see the small bathroom as well?

days.title: Der Mietvertrag und die Nebenkosten
days.objective: Understand the key clauses of a German rental contract and explain why the handover protocol protects you.
days.paretoFocus: Two words in the contract decide how much money you lose later: the notice period, which fixes how long you keep paying, and the handover protocol, which decides whether old damage becomes your bill.
drills: Write three sentences with bevor about things you do before signing a contract.
Explain in German, in two sentences, what the Übergabeprotokoll is for.
Note down every damage you can see in your own room, as a short list with articles.

items: How long is a typical Kündigungsfrist for a tenant?
What arrives once a year for the Nebenkosten, and what can it mean for your account?
Why should you never sign a handover protocol that says everything is fine when it is not?

activities.title: Der Mietvertrag
activities.intro: The contract fixes the rent, the payment date and the notice period. The monthly Nebenkosten are only an advance payment: once a year you get a statement and either pay extra or get money back. The handover protocol is a separate sheet, written on the day you get the keys.

items.hr: Im Mietvertrag steht, wie hoch die Miete ist und wann man sie überweisen muss.
items.en: The rental contract says how high the rent is and when you have to transfer it.
items.note: überweisen, to transfer money, is the normal way rent is paid.

items.hr: Die Kündigungsfrist beträgt für den Mieter meistens drei Monate.
items.en: The notice period for the tenant is usually three months.
items.note: You keep paying rent during the whole Kündigungsfrist.

items.hr: Die Nebenkosten zahlt man monatlich als Vorauszahlung, und einmal im Jahr kommt die Abrechnung.
items.en: You pay the extra costs monthly in advance, and once a year the statement arrives.
items.note: Nach der Abrechnung zahlt man nach oder bekommt Geld zurück.

items.hr: Im Übergabeprotokoll schreibt man alle Schäden auf, bevor man einzieht.
items.en: In the handover protocol you write down all the damage before you move in.
items.note: bevor sends the verb to the end: bevor man einzieht.

items.hr: Das Übergabeprotokoll schützt den Mieter, weil der Vermieter später keine alten Schäden berechnen kann.
items.en: The handover protocol protects the tenant, because the landlord cannot charge for old damage later.
items.note: Ohne Protokoll ist es beim Auszug schwer zu beweisen, was vorher schon kaputt war.

activities.title: Vertrag und Abrechnung
activities.intro: Practise the contract vocabulary and the subordinate clauses you need to explain it.

questions.prompt: Was bedeutet die Kündigungsfrist?
options: Die Zeit zwischen der Kündigung und dem letzten Miettag
Die Summe, die man vor dem Einzug bezahlt
Die Kosten für Heizung, Wasser und Müll
questions.answer: Die Zeit zwischen der Kündigung und dem letzten Miettag
questions.explanation: The Kündigungsfrist is the waiting time after giving notice. You still pay rent for those months, even if you have already moved out.

questions.prompt: Warum ist das Übergabeprotokoll für den Mieter wichtig?
options: Weil dort alle Schäden stehen, die schon vor dem Einzug da waren.
Weil man damit jeden Monat die Miete überweist.
Weil man damit den Internetanschluss bestellt.
Weil man damit die neuen Möbel liefern lässt.
questions.answer: Weil dort alle Schäden stehen, die schon vor dem Einzug da waren.
questions.explanation: The protocol is written proof of the condition of the flat on day one, so old damage cannot be taken out of your deposit at the end.

questions.prompt: Einmal im Jahr bekommt man für die Nebenkosten eine ___, und dann zahlt man nach oder bekommt Geld zurück.
questions.explanation: die Abrechnung is the annual statement that compares your advance payments with the real costs.

questions.prompt: Ich habe den ganzen Vertrag genau gelesen, ___ ich ihn unterschrieben habe.
questions.explanation: bevor introduces the later action and sends its verb to the end of the clause: bevor ich ihn unterschrieben habe.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The notice period is usually three months.'
questions.explanation: betragen is the standard verb for amounts and periods, and the adverb meistens stands directly after the verb.

activities.title: Wir unterschreiben den Vertrag
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are at the table with the landlord to sign the contract; check the notice period, the extra costs and the handover protocol before you sign.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hier ist der Mietvertrag. Bitte lesen Sie ihn in Ruhe durch.
lines.en: Here is the rental contract. Please read it through calmly.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke. Eine Frage: Wie lang ist die Kündigungsfrist für mich?
lines.en: Thank you. One question: how long is the notice period for me?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Drei Monate. Wenn Sie im Januar kündigen, ist der letzte Miettag Ende April.
lines.en: Three months. If you give notice in January, the last day of the tenancy is the end of April.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Verstanden. Und die 190 Euro Nebenkosten sind nur eine Vorauszahlung, oder?
lines.en: Understood. And the 190 euros of extra costs are only an advance payment, right?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Genau. Im Frühling kommt die Abrechnung, meistens bekommen die Mieter etwas zurück.
lines.en: Exactly. The statement comes in spring, and usually the tenants get something back.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut. Machen wir bei der Schlüsselübergabe auch ein Übergabeprotokoll?
lines.en: Good. Will we also do a handover protocol when the keys are handed over?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Natürlich. Wir gehen durch alle Zimmer und schreiben jeden Schaden auf.
lines.en: Of course. We go through all the rooms and write down every damage.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfekt, das schützt uns beide. Dann unterschreibe ich jetzt.
lines.en: Perfect, that protects us both. Then I will sign now.

days.title: Der Umzug
days.objective: Talk about a move in the perfect tense using einziehen, ausziehen and umziehen correctly.
days.paretoFocus: Three separable verbs that look almost identical carry the whole story of a move, and getting haben or sein wrong with them is the mistake native ears notice first.
drills: Tell the story of your last move in five perfect tense sentences.
Sort these into haben or sein: stehen, ziehen, tragen, einziehen, liegen.
Write a short list of what has to be done in the two weeks after moving in.

items: Which auxiliary does stehen take in the perfect in standard German?
What is the difference between einziehen, ausziehen and umziehen?
How soon after moving do you have to register your new address?

activities.title: Einziehen, ausziehen, umziehen
activities.intro: Three separable verbs do most of the work: einziehen is to move in, ausziehen to move out, umziehen to move house. The first two describe a change of place and take sein in the perfect. Position verbs like stehen, liegen and sitzen take haben in standard German.

items.hr: Nächsten Samstag ziehe ich in eine größere Wohnung um.
items.en: Next Saturday I am moving to a bigger flat.
items.note: umziehen is separable: ich ziehe ... um.

items.hr: Wir haben zwanzig schwere Kartons in den dritten Stock getragen.
items.en: We carried twenty heavy boxes up to the third floor.
items.note: in den dritten Stock: accusative, because it answers wohin.

items.hr: Die alte Mieterin ist letzte Woche ausgezogen, und morgen ziehe ich ein.
items.en: The previous tenant moved out last week, and tomorrow I move in.
items.note: ausziehen and einziehen take sein: sie ist ausgezogen.

items.hr: Der neue Schrank hat zwei Stunden im Flur gestanden, weil er nicht durch die Tür gepasst hat.
items.en: The new wardrobe stood in the hallway for two hours because it did not fit through the door.
items.note: hat gestanden, never ist gestanden, in standard German.

items.hr: Nach dem Umzug muss man die neue Adresse innerhalb von zwei Wochen beim Amt anmelden.
items.en: After the move you have to register the new address at the local office within two weeks.
items.note: der Umzug is the noun, umziehen the verb.

activities.title: Umzug im Perfekt
activities.intro: Practise the auxiliaries and the separable verbs of moving house.

questions.prompt: Der schwere Schrank ___ zwei Stunden im Flur gestanden.
questions.explanation: In standard German stehen, liegen and sitzen form the perfect with haben: er hat gestanden, sie hat gelegen, es hat gesessen.

questions.prompt: Wir haben die Kartons in den ___ Stock getragen.
questions.explanation: in plus movement takes the accusative, and a masculine noun after den needs the ending -en on the adjective.

questions.prompt: Vor dem Umzug packt man alle Sachen in große ___ aus Pappe.
questions.explanation: der Karton, plural die Kartons, is the cardboard box everything gets packed into.

questions.prompt: Die alte Mieterin ist am Montag ausgezogen, und am Dienstag bin ich ___.
questions.explanation: einziehen is the opposite of ausziehen and also takes sein, because it describes a change of place.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Next Saturday I am moving to a bigger flat.'
questions.explanation: The time phrase takes first position, so verb and subject swap, and the separable prefix um goes right to the end.

activities.title: Am Tag des Umzugs
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend is helping you move and asks how the day went; use the perfect tense with the right auxiliaries.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und, wie war der Umzug? Habt ihr alles geschafft?
lines.en: So, how was the move? Did you manage everything?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Fast. Wir haben zwanzig schwere Kartons in den dritten Stock getragen.
lines.en: Almost. We carried twenty heavy boxes up to the third floor.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ohne Aufzug? Das tut mir leid. Und die Möbel?
lines.en: Without a lift? I am sorry to hear that. And the furniture?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Der große Schrank hat zwei Stunden im Flur gestanden, weil er nicht durch die Tür gepasst hat.
lines.en: The big wardrobe stood in the hallway for two hours because it did not fit through the door.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was habt ihr dann gemacht?
lines.en: And what did you do then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wir haben ihn auseinandergebaut. Um zehn Uhr abends bin ich endlich eingezogen.
lines.en: We took it apart. At ten in the evening I finally moved in.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Denk daran: Du musst die neue Adresse innerhalb von zwei Wochen anmelden.
lines.en: Remember: you have to register the new address within two weeks.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke, das mache ich am Montag. Heute stelle ich nur das Bett auf.
lines.en: Thanks, I will do that on Monday. Today I am only putting up the bed.

days.title: Möbel kaufen und liefern lassen
days.objective: Order furniture and arrange delivery and assembly using lassen plus an infinitive.
days.paretoFocus: lassen plus infinitive is how German says 'have something done', and it turns up every time you order, repair or organise anything without doing it yourself.
drills: Write five sentences with lassen about things you do not do yourself.
Describe three pieces of furniture in your home with an adjective before each noun.
Practise asking for a delivery date and the price of assembly.

items: What is the difference between Ich baue den Schrank auf and Ich lasse den Schrank aufbauen?
Where does the infinitive go in a sentence with lassen?
Which two extra services usually cost money when you buy furniture?

activities.title: Etwas machen lassen
activities.intro: When somebody else does the work for you, German uses lassen with a bare infinitive at the end of the sentence: Ich lasse den Schrank liefern. The pattern works exactly like a modal verb, but the meaning is 'I have it done', not 'I do it'.

items.hr: Ich lasse den schweren Schrank nach Hause liefern.
items.en: I am having the heavy wardrobe delivered to my home.
items.note: lassen plus infinitive: the infinitive liefern stands at the end.

items.hr: Wir lassen die neue Küche aufbauen, weil wir kein Werkzeug haben.
items.en: We are having the new kitchen assembled because we have no tools.
items.note: aufbauen, to assemble, is the standard word in furniture shops.

items.hr: Die Lieferung kostet 49 Euro, und der Aufbau kostet 89 Euro extra.
items.en: The delivery costs 49 euros and the assembly costs another 89 euros.
items.note: die Lieferung and der Aufbau are usually two separate items on the bill.

items.hr: Ich habe einen breiten Tisch und vier bequeme Stühle bestellt.
items.en: I have ordered a wide table and four comfortable chairs.
items.note: einen breiten Tisch (masculine accusative), vier bequeme Stühle (plural).

items.hr: Die Lieferung dauert zwei bis drei Wochen, und Sie bekommen vorher einen Termin.
items.en: The delivery takes two to three weeks, and you get an appointment beforehand.
items.note: Ask: Können Sie mir sagen, wann die Lieferung kommt?

activities.title: Liefern und aufbauen
activities.intro: Practise lassen plus infinitive and the adjective endings for furniture.

questions.prompt: Ich baue den Schrank nicht selbst auf. Ich ___ ihn aufbauen.
questions.explanation: lassen plus infinitive means that somebody else does the work for you. The infinitive aufbauen has no zu.

questions.prompt: Ich habe einen breiten Tisch und vier ___ Stühle bestellt.
questions.explanation: In the plural accusative without an article the adjective takes -e after a number: vier bequeme Stühle.

questions.prompt: Der Tisch ist viel zu groß für mein Auto, deshalb ___ ich ihn liefern.
questions.explanation: The infinitive liefern at the end needs a form of lassen in second position: deshalb lasse ich ihn liefern.

questions.prompt: Die Firma bringt die Möbel bis zur Wohnungstür. Dieser Service heißt die ___.
questions.explanation: die Lieferung is the delivery. The assembly on top of it is called der Aufbau.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We are having the new kitchen assembled.'
questions.explanation: lassen behaves like a modal verb: it stands in second position and pushes the infinitive aufbauen to the end.

activities.title: Im Möbelgeschäft
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are buying a table and chairs and arranging delivery and assembly; use lassen plus infinitive.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag, kann ich Ihnen helfen?
lines.en: Hello, can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich möchte diesen breiten Tisch und vier bequeme Stühle bestellen.
lines.en: Yes, I would like to order this wide table and four comfortable chairs.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gerne. Nehmen Sie die Sachen gleich mit oder sollen wir liefern?
lines.en: Gladly. Will you take the items with you now or shall we deliver?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Der Tisch ist zu groß für mein Auto. Ich lasse alles nach Hause liefern.
lines.en: The table is too big for my car. I will have everything delivered to my home.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Die Lieferung kostet 49 Euro. Möchten Sie die Möbel auch aufbauen lassen?
lines.en: The delivery costs 49 euros. Would you also like to have the furniture assembled?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, bitte. Ich habe kein Werkzeug. Was kostet der Aufbau?
lines.en: Yes, please. I have no tools. What does the assembly cost?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: 89 Euro. Die Lieferung dauert zwei bis drei Wochen.
lines.en: 89 euros. The delivery takes two to three weeks.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut. Können Sie mir sagen, wann ich den Termin bekomme?
lines.en: Good. Can you tell me when I will get the appointment?

days.title: Die Nachbarn und die Hausordnung
days.objective: Understand the house rules and speak politely with neighbours about noise, cleaning and shared spaces.
days.paretoFocus: The house rules are the unwritten social contract of a German building, and one friendly sentence before a party prevents most of the conflicts that end in letters from the landlord.
drills: Write the five rules you think should be in a good Hausordnung.
Practise saying sorry for noise and offering a solution, in two sentences.
Introduce yourself to a new neighbour in three sentences with a reflexive verb.

items: When are the usual quiet hours in a German building?
What does man sagt Bescheid mean and when do you do it?
Which case do you need after sagen for the person you speak to?

activities.title: Die Hausordnung und die Ruhezeit
activities.intro: Every building has a Hausordnung, a sheet of rules that is usually part of the contract. The most important part is the Ruhezeit, the quiet hours. Telling neighbours in advance is normal and costs nothing: man sagt Bescheid.

items.hr: In der Hausordnung steht, wann die Ruhezeit im Haus beginnt.
items.en: The house rules say when the quiet time in the building starts.
items.note: die Hausordnung is usually attached to the rental contract.

items.hr: Zwischen 22 Uhr und 6 Uhr und den ganzen Sonntag muss man leise sein.
items.en: Between 10 pm and 6 am and all day Sunday you have to be quiet.
items.note: Sunday is a rest day for noisy work like drilling or washing machines in many buildings.

items.hr: Im Treppenhaus putzt jede Woche eine andere Familie.
items.en: In the stairwell a different family cleans each week.
items.note: das Treppenhaus, the shared stairwell, is often cleaned in turns.

items.hr: Wenn ich eine laute Party mache, sage ich meinen Nachbarn vorher Bescheid.
items.en: If I have a loud party, I tell my neighbours in advance.
items.note: jemandem Bescheid sagen takes the dative: meinen Nachbarn.

items.hr: Unser neuer Nachbar hat sich gestern im Treppenhaus vorgestellt.
items.en: Our new neighbour introduced himself in the stairwell yesterday.
items.note: sich vorstellen is reflexive: er hat sich vorgestellt.

activities.title: Regeln im Haus
activities.intro: Practise the vocabulary of house rules and the dative for the person you address.

questions.prompt: Was ist die Ruhezeit?
options: Die Zeit, in der man im Haus leise sein muss.
Die Zeit, in der man die Miete überweist.
Die Zeit, in der der Müll abgeholt wird.
questions.answer: Die Zeit, in der man im Haus leise sein muss.
questions.explanation: Ruhezeit means the hours when loud noise is not allowed, typically at night and on Sundays.

questions.prompt: Vor der Party sage ich ___ Bescheid.
questions.explanation: jemandem Bescheid sagen needs the dative, and the dative plural of mein is meinen.

questions.prompt: Wenn ich abends eine laute Party mache, sage ich meinen Nachbarn vorher ___.
questions.explanation: Bescheid sagen is a fixed expression meaning to let somebody know. Bescheid is always capitalised.

questions.prompt: Die Regeln für alle Bewohner des Hauses stehen in der ___.
questions.explanation: die Hausordnung is the set of building rules. It is usually part of the rental contract and therefore binding.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Our new neighbour introduced himself yesterday.'
questions.explanation: After unser the masculine adjective takes -er, and the reflexive pronoun sich stands directly after the auxiliary.

activities.title: Ein Gespräch im Treppenhaus
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You meet the neighbour from downstairs in the stairwell and talk about the house rules and your party on Saturday.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Morgen! Sie sind die neue Mieterin aus dem dritten Stock, oder?
lines.en: Good morning! You are the new tenant from the third floor, aren't you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, genau. Ich bin letzte Woche eingezogen. Ich wollte mich noch vorstellen.
lines.en: Yes, exactly. I moved in last week. I still wanted to introduce myself.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sehr nett. Haben Sie die Hausordnung schon gelesen?
lines.en: Very nice. Have you already read the house rules?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ab 22 Uhr ist Ruhezeit, und das Treppenhaus putzt jede Woche eine andere Familie.
lines.en: Yes, quiet time starts at 10 pm, and a different family cleans the stairwell each week.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Richtig. Nächste Woche sind Sie dran, ich zeige Ihnen den Plan.
lines.en: Correct. Next week it is your turn, I will show you the schedule.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke. Am Samstag habe ich Gäste. Ich sage Ihnen lieber vorher Bescheid.
lines.en: Thank you. On Saturday I have guests. I would rather let you know in advance.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das ist sehr freundlich. Bis zehn ist kein Problem, danach bitte leiser.
lines.en: That is very kind. Until ten it is no problem, after that a bit quieter please.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Abgemacht. Dann machen wir um zehn die Fenster zu und die Musik leise.
lines.en: Agreed. Then at ten we will close the windows and turn the music down.

days.title: Mülltrennung in Deutschland
days.objective: Sort household waste into the right bins and explain the system, including the deposit on bottles.
days.paretoFocus: Waste separation is the everyday rule newcomers break first and neighbours comment on fastest, and the vocabulary is small enough to master in one lesson.
drills: Go through your own rubbish and name the correct bin for each item in German.
Explain the deposit system on bottles in three German sentences.
Write down which colours the glass container is divided into and what belongs where.

items: Which bin do plastic and metal packaging go into?
What do you do with a bottle that has Pfand on it?
