The nouns come in pairs that are worth learning together, der Abflug with die Ankunft and das Handgepäck with das Aufgabegepäck. Everything is announced with the noun first, so recognising the noun is half the work.

items.hr: Ich möchte diesen Koffer aufgeben, das Handgepäck nehme ich mit an Bord.
items.en: I would like to check this suitcase in, I am taking the hand luggage on board.
items.note: aufgeben, to hand a bag over for the hold

items.hr: Die Bordkarte habe ich auf dem Handy, den Ausweis zeige ich an der Kontrolle.
items.en: I have the boarding pass on my phone, I show the identity card at the check.
items.note: die Bordkarte, der Ausweis

items.hr: Der Schalter öffnet zwei Stunden vor dem Abflug und schließt vierzig Minuten davor.
items.en: The desk opens two hours before departure and closes forty minutes before it.
items.note: der Abflug and die Ankunft, departure and arrival

items.hr: An der Sicherheitskontrolle muss ich die Flüssigkeiten und den Laptop herausnehmen.
items.en: At the security check I have to take the liquids and the laptop out.
items.note: herausnehmen is separable, ich nehme sie heraus

items.hr: Der Flug hat eine Stunde Verspätung, und wir gehen an einem anderen Flugsteig an Bord.
items.en: The flight is one hour late, and we board at a different gate.
items.note: der Flugsteig, the gate

activities.title: Vom Schalter bis an Bord
activities.intro: Practise the vocabulary of check-in, security and boarding, separable prefixes included.

questions.prompt: You want to hand your suitcase over for the hold. What do you say?
questions.explanation: aufgeben is the fixed verb for checking luggage in. mitnehmen means to take it with you and abholen means to collect it later.

questions.prompt: Which noun is the boarding pass?
questions.explanation: die Bordkarte is the pass that gets you on board. die Fahrkarte is for trains and buses, and der Ausweis is your identity document.

questions.prompt: Complete with the noun for the departure of a plane: Der Schalter öffnet zwei Stunden vor dem ___.
questions.explanation: der Abflug is used for planes, while die Abfahrt is used for trains and buses. After vor dem it stays unchanged in the dative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the opposite of der Abflug: Die ___ ist um 21:15.
questions.explanation: die Ankunft is the arrival, the counterpart of der Abflug, and it is feminine.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I would like to check this suitcase in.'
questions.explanation: The modal möchte takes second position and pushes the infinitive aufgeben to the end, with the accusative diesen Koffer in between.

activities.title: Am Schalter und an der Kontrolle
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are checking in a bag that turns out to be too heavy, and the gate has changed.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Morgen, die Bordkarte und den Ausweis, bitte.
lines.en: Good morning, the boarding pass and the identity card, please.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hier, bitte. Ich möchte diesen Koffer aufgeben.
lines.en: Here you are. I would like to check this suitcase in.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Der Koffer wiegt vierundzwanzig Kilo, das ist leider zu schwer.
lines.en: The suitcase weighs twenty four kilos, unfortunately that is too heavy.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann nehme ich die Bücher heraus und stecke sie ins Handgepäck.
lines.en: Then I will take the books out and put them in my hand luggage.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut so. Der Abflug ist um 6:40, aber der Flugsteig wurde geändert.
lines.en: That works. Departure is at 6:40, but the gate has been changed.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: An welchem Flugsteig gehen wir jetzt an Bord?
lines.en: At which gate do we board now?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: An B 12. Gehen Sie bitte gleich durch die Sicherheitskontrolle.
lines.en: At B 12. Please go through the security check straight away.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vielen Dank, dann packe ich die Flüssigkeiten schon nach oben.
lines.en: Thank you, then I will pack the liquids at the top already.

days.title: Das Hotel, buchen und einchecken
days.objective: Reserve a room, confirm a booking at reception, ask what is included and make a polite request about the room using the Konjunktiv II.
days.paretoFocus: Reception conversations follow a script, name, room type, nights, breakfast, checkout, so learning the script means you can complete the whole stay without improvising.
drills: Book a room out loud three times: single, double, and one with a bathroom.
Turn three direct requests into polite ones with the Konjunktiv II.
Say the full check in from the greeting to the question about breakfast.

items: Why is there no ge- in the past participle of reservieren?
How do you ask whether breakfast is included in the price?
Which verb do you use for vacating the room on the day you leave?

activities.title: An der Rezeption
activities.intro: A booking is confirmed with auf den Namen plus the surname, which is a fixed expression worth memorising. Verbs ending in ieren, such as reservieren, form their participle without ge-, so it is reserviert and not gereserviert. Requests about the room stay polite through the Konjunktiv II, könnten and wäre.

items.hr: Ich habe ein Einzelzimmer für drei Nächte reserviert, auf den Namen Meier.
items.en: I have reserved a single room for three nights, under the name Meier.
items.note: reservieren -> reserviert, no ge- with verbs in ieren

items.hr: Ist das Frühstück im Preis inbegriffen?
items.en: Is breakfast included in the price?
items.note: inbegriffen, included

items.hr: Haben Sie noch ein Doppelzimmer mit Bad frei?
items.en: Do you still have a double room with a bathroom available?
items.note: frei haben, to have available

items.hr: Wann muss ich am Abreisetag das Zimmer räumen?
items.en: When do I have to vacate the room on the day of departure?
items.note: die Anreise and die Abreise, arrival and departure day

items.hr: Könnten Sie mir bitte ein ruhiges Zimmer zum Hof geben?
items.en: Could you please give me a quiet room facing the courtyard?
items.note: Konjunktiv II könnten makes the request polite without weakening it

activities.title: Ein Zimmer reservieren
activities.intro: Practise the booking script and the polite forms that go with it.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly says that you have reserved a room?
questions.explanation: reservieren takes haben, and verbs ending in ieren never add ge- in the participle, so the form is reserviert.

questions.prompt: Which question asks whether breakfast is part of the price?
options: Ist das Frühstück im Preis inbegriffen?
Gibt es das Frühstück um acht Uhr?
Wie viel kostet das Frühstück extra?
questions.answer: Ist das Frühstück im Preis inbegriffen?
questions.explanation: inbegriffen means included in something already paid for. The other questions ask about the time, the place and an extra charge.

questions.prompt: Complete the polite request with the Konjunktiv II of können: ___ Sie mir bitte ein ruhiges Zimmer geben?
questions.explanation: Könnten is the Konjunktiv II form used for polite requests, and it is capitalised here because it opens the question.

questions.prompt: Complete: Haben Sie noch ein Doppelzimmer mit Bad ___?
questions.explanation: frei haben is the fixed expression for a room being available. It stays uninflected because it works as an adverb here.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I have reserved a single room for three nights.'
questions.explanation: In the perfect the auxiliary habe stays in second position and the participle reserviert closes the sentence, after the time phrase.

activities.title: Der Check-in am Abend
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You arrive with a booking and want to confirm the details and improve your room.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Abend, haben Sie bei uns reserviert?
lines.en: Good evening, have you made a reservation with us?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ein Einzelzimmer für drei Nächte, auf den Namen Weber.
lines.en: Yes, a single room for three nights, under the name Weber.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich sehe die Reservierung, Zimmer 214 im zweiten Stock.
lines.en: I can see the reservation, room 214 on the second floor.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ist das Frühstück im Preis inbegriffen?
lines.en: Is breakfast included in the price?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, von sieben bis halb elf im Raum neben der Rezeption.
lines.en: Yes, from seven to half past ten in the room next to reception.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Könnten Sie mir bitte ein ruhiges Zimmer zum Hof geben?
lines.en: Could you please give me a quiet room facing the courtyard?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Einen Moment, ich schaue nach. Zimmer 305 liegt hinten und ist frei.
lines.en: One moment, I will check. Room 305 is at the back and is available.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sehr gern, das nehme ich. Wann muss ich am Abreisetag das Zimmer räumen?
lines.en: Gladly, I will take it. When do I have to vacate the room on the day I leave?

days.title: Sich beschweren, wenn etwas nicht stimmt
days.objective: Complain about a problem clearly and politely, naming what is wrong rather than blaming a person, and asking for a fix with the Konjunktiv II.
days.paretoFocus: German complaints are direct about the fact and polite in form, so once you have the impersonal opening and the Konjunktiv II request you can raise any problem without sounding either rude or apologetic.
drills: Name three problems in a room out loud, each in one sentence that says what is wrong, not who is to blame.
Turn each of those three sentences into a polite request for a fix using the Konjunktiv II.
Complain once about something without any apology at the start of the sentence.

items: Which preposition and which case follow sich beschweren?
What is the impersonal way of saying that something is not right?
Which verb do you use when a device does not work?

activities.title: Höflich, aber deutlich
activities.intro: German complaints stay polite through grammar, not through apology. The opening is impersonal, etwas stimmt nicht names the problem without accusing anybody, and the request that follows uses the Konjunktiv II. Do not start with a long apology for existing, because a German listener reads that as unclear rather than as friendly.

items.hr: Entschuldigung, hier stimmt etwas nicht.
items.en: Excuse me, something is not right here.
items.note: impersonal opening, states the problem without blaming a person

items.hr: Das Zimmer ist leider nicht sauber, könnten Sie das bitte prüfen?
items.en: Unfortunately the room is not clean, could you please check that?
items.note: leider softens the fact, könnten carries the request

items.hr: Ich würde gern mit der zuständigen Person sprechen.
items.en: I would like to speak to the person responsible.
items.note: würde plus infinitive, polite and neutral

items.hr: Die Heizung funktioniert seit gestern nicht.
items.en: The heating has not been working since yesterday.
items.note: funktionieren for devices, and seit plus present tense where English uses a perfect

items.hr: Ich möchte mich über den Lärm im Nachbarzimmer beschweren.
items.en: I would like to complain about the noise in the room next door.
items.note: sich beschweren über plus accusative

activities.title: Ein Problem melden
activities.intro: Practise the impersonal opening and the polite request that follows it.

questions.prompt: Which preposition and case follow sich beschweren?
questions.explanation: sich beschweren always takes über with the accusative, so it is über den Lärm and not über dem Lärm.

questions.prompt: Which opening is polite and clear at the same time in German?
options: Es tut mir sehr leid, dass ich Sie überhaupt störe.
questions.explanation: The impersonal form names the problem without accusing the listener. A long apology sounds vague, and a direct accusation sounds aggressive.

questions.prompt: Complete the reflexive pronoun: Ich möchte ___ über den Lärm beschweren.
questions.explanation: sich beschweren is reflexive, and the pronoun for ich in the accusative is mich.

questions.prompt: Complete with the verb used for machines and devices: Die Heizung ___ seit gestern nicht.
questions.explanation: German uses funktionieren for devices. arbeiten is used for people, so eine Heizung arbeitet nicht sounds wrong.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Could you please check that?'
questions.explanation: The Konjunktiv II form Könnten opens the question, and the infinitive prüfen closes it, with bitte just before the infinitive.

activities.title: Ein Problem im Zimmer
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You call reception about two problems and ask for both to be dealt with.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Rezeption, guten Abend. Was kann ich für Sie tun?
lines.en: Reception, good evening. What can I do for you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Guten Abend. Entschuldigung, in Zimmer 305 stimmt etwas nicht.
lines.en: Good evening. Excuse me, something is not right in room 305.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was genau ist denn das Problem?
lines.en: What exactly is the problem?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Heizung funktioniert seit gestern nicht, und es ist sehr kalt.
lines.en: The heating has not been working since yesterday, and it is very cold.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das tut mir leid, ich schicke gleich jemanden hoch.
lines.en: I am sorry, I will send somebody up right away.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke. Ich möchte mich außerdem über den Lärm im Nachbarzimmer beschweren.
lines.en: Thank you. I would also like to complain about the noise in the room next door.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich spreche mit den Gästen. Soll ich Ihnen ein anderes Zimmer geben?
lines.en: I will speak to the guests. Shall I give you a different room?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, das wäre mir lieber. Ein Zimmer im hinteren Teil des Hauses wäre ideal.
lines.en: Yes, I would prefer that. A room at the back of the building would be ideal.

days.title: Im Restaurant reklamieren
days.objective: Say that the wrong dish arrived, that the food is not as it should be, or that the bill is wrong, and get it corrected without a scene.
days.paretoFocus: Three things go wrong in restaurants, the order, the food itself and the bill, and each one has a short standard sentence that native speakers actually use.
drills: Say three things that can be wrong with a dish and one thing that can be wrong with the bill.
Ask for the same problem to be fixed twice, once directly and once with the Konjunktiv II.
Say how long you have been waiting for something, using the right preposition and tense.

items: How do you say that what arrived is not what you ordered?
Which case follows warten auf?
Which small word softens a complaint just before the bad news?

activities.title: Wenn die Bestellung nicht stimmt
activities.intro: The key sentence for a wrong order puts so before the negative, das habe ich so nicht bestellt, which means not in this form rather than not at all. For the bill, hätte gern with a participle lets you ask for something to be done without ordering anybody around. Waiting is expressed with warten auf plus accusative, and seit plus present tense where English would use a perfect.

items.hr: Entschuldigung, das habe ich so nicht bestellt.
items.en: Excuse me, that is not what I ordered.
items.note: so nicht means not in this form, softer than a flat nicht

items.hr: Die Suppe ist leider kalt, könnten Sie sie bitte noch einmal warm machen?
items.en: Unfortunately the soup is cold, could you please heat it up again?
items.note: leider plus a Konjunktiv II request is the standard pair

items.hr: Ich hätte gern die Rechnung noch einmal geprüft.
items.en: I would like the bill to be checked again.
items.note: hätte gern plus participle asks for an action politely

items.hr: Das Fleisch ist mir zu durch, ich hatte es blutig bestellt.
items.en: The meat is too well done for me, I had ordered it rare.
items.note: blutig, rosa, durch, the three degrees

items.hr: Wir warten seit vierzig Minuten auf das Essen.
items.en: We have been waiting for the food for forty minutes.
items.note: warten auf plus accusative, and seit with the present tense

activities.title: Die Rechnung und das Essen
activities.intro: Practise the standard sentences for a wrong order, cold food and a wrong bill.

questions.prompt: The dish that arrived is not the one you ordered. What do you say?
options: Das habe ich so nicht bestellt.
questions.answer: Das habe ich so nicht bestellt.
questions.explanation: so nicht bestellt points at the mistake in the order. The other sentences complain about taste, price, or say that you did not eat it.

questions.prompt: Which case follows the verb combination warten auf?
questions.explanation: warten auf is one of the fixed verb and preposition pairs that always take the accusative, so it is auf das Essen.

questions.prompt: Complete the preposition: Wir warten seit vierzig Minuten ___ das Essen.
questions.explanation: The fixed pair is warten auf plus accusative, which is why das Essen keeps the accusative article das.

questions.prompt: Complete with the word that softens bad news: Die Suppe ist ___ kalt.
questions.explanation: leider marks the statement as unwelcome news rather than an accusation, and it stands directly before the adjective here.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'There is one drink too many on the bill here.'
questions.explanation: Hier takes first position, so the verb steht follows immediately, and auf der Rechnung is dative because it describes a position.

activities.title: Die Bestellung stimmt nicht
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. The wrong dish arrives, the soup is cold and the bill has an extra item.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: So, einmal die Nudeln mit Gemüse und einmal die Suppe.
lines.en: There you go, one pasta with vegetables and one soup.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Entschuldigung, das habe ich so nicht bestellt, ich hatte den Salat.
lines.en: Excuse me, that is not what I ordered, I had the salad.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Oh, das tut mir leid, ich bringe Ihnen sofort den Salat.
lines.en: Oh, I am sorry, I will bring you the salad right away.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke. Und die Suppe ist leider kalt, könnten Sie sie bitte warm machen?
lines.en: Thank you. And unfortunately the soup is cold, could you please heat it up?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Natürlich, ich nehme sie gleich mit.
lines.en: Of course, I will take it back with me straight away.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Außerdem hätte ich gern die Rechnung noch einmal geprüft.
lines.en: I would also like the bill to be checked again.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gern, was stimmt denn nicht?
lines.en: Gladly, what is not right?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hier steht ein Getränk zu viel, wir hatten nur zwei Wasser.
lines.en: There is one drink too many here, we only had two waters.

days.title: Etwas umtauschen und reklamieren
days.objective: Take something back to a shop, tell the difference between an exchange and a refund, and describe a fault so that the shop has to act.
days.paretoFocus: The outcome of a return depends entirely on one distinction, faulty goods against a change of mind, and naming the fault in German is what decides which conversation you get.
drills: Say out loud what you take to the shop when you want to exchange something.
Describe one faulty item and one item you simply no longer want, and say which one the shop must take back.
Ask for your money back in one sentence and for an exchange in another.

items: What are the two common German words for a till receipt?
What is the difference between umtauschen and zurückgeben?
Does a shop have to take goods back when the customer has simply changed their mind?

activities.title: Umtauschen oder zurückgeben
activities.intro: Two verbs look similar but do different jobs, umtauschen means swapping the item for another one and zurückgeben means handing it back and getting money. The word that unlocks your rights is der Mangel, a defect, because faulty goods must be dealt with while a change of mind is only ever a favour from the shop. Always bring the receipt, either der Kassenbon or die Quittung.

items.hr: Ich möchte diese Jacke umtauschen, hier ist der Kassenbon.
items.en: I would like to exchange this jacket, here is the receipt.
items.note: der Kassenbon and die Quittung both mean receipt

items.hr: Der Reißverschluss ist kaputt, das ist ein Mangel.
items.en: The zip is broken, that is a defect.
items.note: der Mangel, the word that makes the shop responsible

items.hr: Kann ich die Schuhe zurückgeben und das Geld zurückbekommen?
items.en: Can I return the shoes and get my money back?
items.note: zurückgeben for a refund, umtauschen for a swap

items.hr: Auf das Gerät habe ich zwei Jahre Garantie.
items.en: I have a two year guarantee on the device.
items.note: die Garantie, and Garantie auf plus accusative

items.hr: Wenn die Ware in Ordnung ist, muss der Laden sie nicht zurücknehmen.
items.en: If the goods are fine, the shop does not have to take them back.
items.note: changing your mind gives you no claim, a defect does

activities.title: Zurück ins Geschäft
activities.intro: Practise naming a fault and asking for the outcome you actually want.

questions.prompt: Which word is the slip of paper you get at the till?
questions.explanation: der Kassenbon is the till receipt, and die Quittung is its common synonym. der Vertrag is a contract and das Angebot is an offer.

questions.prompt: You want your money back, not a different item. What do you ask?
options: Kann ich die Schuhe zurückgeben und das Geld zurückbekommen?
questions.answer: Kann ich die Schuhe zurückgeben und das Geld zurückbekommen?
questions.explanation: umtauschen only swaps the item, while zurückgeben plus das Geld zurückbekommen asks for the refund.

questions.prompt: Complete with the noun for a defect: Der Reißverschluss ist kaputt, das ist ein ___.
questions.explanation: der Mangel is the legal word for a defect in goods, and it is masculine, so the article is ein.

questions.prompt: Complete: Auf das Gerät habe ich zwei Jahre ___.
questions.explanation: die Garantie is used without an article in this fixed expression, and the preposition auf takes the accusative here.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Could you please exchange that?'
questions.explanation: The Konjunktiv II opens the question and the separable verb umtauschen stays whole as an infinitive at the end.

activities.title: Im Geschäft reklamieren
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You return a jacket with a broken zip and ask for a refund rather than a swap.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag, kann ich Ihnen helfen?
lines.en: Hello, can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich möchte diese Jacke umtauschen, der Reißverschluss ist kaputt.
lines.en: Yes, I would like to exchange this jacket, the zip is broken.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Haben Sie den Kassenbon dabei?
lines.en: Do you have the receipt with you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, hier. Ich habe die Jacke vor zehn Tagen gekauft.
lines.en: Yes, here it is. I bought the jacket ten days ago.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bei einem Mangel ist das kein Problem. Möchten Sie dieselbe Jacke noch einmal?
lines.en: With a defect that is no problem. Would you like the same jacket again?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Lieber das Geld zurück, wenn das möglich ist.
lines.en: I would rather have the money back, if that is possible.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bei einem Mangel geht das. Wäre die Ware in Ordnung, könnten wir sie nicht zurücknehmen.
lines.en: With a defect that is possible. If the goods were fine, we could not take them back.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das verstehe ich. Vielen Dank für die schnelle Hilfe.
lines.en: I understand that. Thank you for the quick help.

days.title: Von einer Reise erzählen
days.objective: Tell the story of a trip as one connected narrative, using the Perfekt for the events and the Präteritum for sein, haben and the modal verbs, linked with zuerst, dann, danach and am Ende.
days.paretoFocus: Nobody asks for isolated past tense sentences, they ask how the trip was, so being able to run six or seven sentences together in the right tenses is what makes you sound like a speaker rather than a learner.
drills: Tell the story of your last trip in six connected sentences, not six separate ones.
Go through your story again and check that the background verbs stand in the Präteritum, not the Perfekt.
Add one reason with weil and one consequence with deshalb to the same story.

items: Which auxiliary does sitzen take in standard German, haben or sein?
Which verbs stay in the Präteritum even in spoken narration?
Which words connect the steps of a story in order?

activities.title: Die Geschichte einer Reise
activities.intro: In everyday spoken German the events of a story go in the Perfekt, but sein, haben and the modals normally stay in the Präteritum even in speech. The story holds together through order words such as zuerst, danach and am Ende, plus weil and deshalb for reasons and results. In standard German of Germany, sitzen, stehen and liegen form their perfect with haben.

items.hr: Zuerst sind wir mit dem Zug nach Dresden gefahren, die Fahrt hat vier Stunden gedauert.
items.en: First we travelled to Dresden by train, the journey took four hours.
items.note: fahren takes sein, dauern takes haben

items.hr: Das Wetter war schlecht, deshalb hatten wir am ersten Tag wenig Lust auf die Stadt.
items.en: The weather was bad, so on the first day we did not feel much like seeing the city.
items.note: war and hatten stay in the Präteritum even when speaking

items.hr: Wir wollten ins Museum, aber es war an dem Tag geschlossen.
items.en: We wanted to go to the museum, but it was closed that day.
items.note: modal verbs in the Präteritum, wollten, konnten, mussten

items.hr: Danach haben wir stundenlang am Fluss gesessen und einfach nur geredet.
items.en: After that we sat by the river for hours and just talked.
items.note: hat gesessen, not ist gesessen, in the standard German of Germany

items.hr: Am Ende hat sich die Reise gelohnt, weil wir nette Leute kennengelernt haben.
items.en: In the end the trip was worth it, because we got to know nice people.
items.note: sich lohnen, and weil sends the finite verb to the end

activities.title: Erzählen im Perfekt und Präteritum
activities.intro: Practise choosing the right tense and the right auxiliary inside a connected story.

questions.prompt: Which is the standard German of Germany for 'we sat by the river'?
questions.explanation: In the northern standard, sitzen, stehen and liegen form the perfect with haben. The form with sein is heard in the south but is not the standard taught here.

questions.prompt: In spoken narration, which verb normally appears in the Präteritum rather than the Perfekt?
questions.explanation: sein, haben and the modal verbs keep their Präteritum forms in speech, so a speaker says es war and not es ist gewesen in a normal story.

questions.prompt: Complete with the Präteritum of the modal verb for wanting: Wir ___ ins Museum, aber es war geschlossen.
questions.explanation: The Präteritum of wollen for wir is wollten, without an umlaut, and modal verbs prefer this form over the Perfekt in narration.

questions.prompt: Complete the past participle: Die Fahrt hat vier Stunden ___.
questions.explanation: dauern is a regular weak verb, so the participle is gedauert, and it takes haben even though it describes a length of time.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'First we travelled to Dresden by train.'
questions.explanation: Zuerst takes first position, so the auxiliary sind follows immediately and the subject wir comes after it, with the participle gefahren at the end.

activities.title: Wie war die Reise?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Back from your trip, you tell it to a friend as one story, not as separate facts.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Du warst doch letzte Woche unterwegs, wie war die Reise?
lines.en: You were away last week, weren't you, how was the trip?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ganz gut. Zuerst sind wir mit dem Zug nach Dresden gefahren, die Fahrt hat vier Stunden gedauert.
lines.en: Quite good. First we travelled to Dresden by train, the journey took four hours.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vier Stunden? Hattet ihr wenigstens gutes Wetter?
lines.en: Four hours? Did you at least have good weather?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, es hat die ganze Zeit geregnet, deshalb hatten wir am ersten Tag wenig Lust.
lines.en: No, it rained the whole time, so on the first day we did not feel like much.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was habt ihr dann gemacht?
lines.en: And what did you do then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wir wollten ins Museum, aber es war geschlossen, also haben wir stundenlang im Café gesessen.
lines.en: We wanted to go to the museum, but it was closed, so we sat in a cafe for hours.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das klingt trotzdem entspannt.
lines.en: That sounds relaxing anyway.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Ende hat sich die Reise gelohnt, weil wir dort nette Leute kennengelernt haben.
lines.en: In the end the trip was worth it, because we got to know nice people there.

days.title: Formelle und informelle E-Mails
days.objective: Write the same message twice, once formally to a stranger and once informally to a friend, using the correct opening, address form and sign-off for each.
days.paretoFocus: Almost every written task an adult learner faces is an email, and readers judge the register in the first three words and the last three, so getting the frame right matters more than the middle.
drills: Write one formal email of four sentences asking a landlady to repair the heating.
Rewrite the same four sentences as a message to a friend, changing every polite form to the familiar one.
Take three emails you would realistically send this month and write only their opening line and sign-off, deciding for each which register it needs.

items: How do you open a formal email to a woman called Weber, and what follows the comma?
Which two sign-offs suit a friend, and which one suits a company?
If you start an email with the polite address form, how far through the email do you keep it?

activities.title: Zwei Register, eine Nachricht
activities.intro: German email splits sharply into formal and informal, and the two never mix. A formal email opens with Sehr geehrte or Sehr geehrter plus the surname, uses Sie from the first line to the last, and ends with Mit freundlichen Grüßen. An informal one opens with Hallo or Liebe plus the first name, uses du throughout, and ends with Viele Grüße or Liebe Grüße.

items.hr: Sehr geehrte Frau Weber, ich schreibe Ihnen wegen der Heizung in meiner Wohnung.
items.en: Dear Ms Weber, I am writing to you about the heating in my flat.
items.note: After the comma the next word stays lower case. Use Sehr geehrter Herr Weber for a man.

items.hr: Hallo Anna, ich schreibe dir wegen der Heizung in meiner Wohnung.
items.en: Hi Anna, I am writing to you about the heating in my flat.
items.note: Same message, familiar register: Hallo plus first name, and dir instead of Ihnen.

items.hr: Könnten Sie mir bitte sagen, wann der Handwerker kommt?
items.en: Could you please tell me when the workman is coming?
items.note: Formal email keeps Sie in every single sentence. Der Handwerker, der Termin, die Wohnung.

items.hr: Kannst du mir bitte sagen, wann der Handwerker kommt?
items.en: Can you please tell me when the workman is coming?
items.note: Informal email keeps du in every single sentence. Never switch halfway.

items.hr: Zum Schluss schreibe ich: Mit freundlichen Grüßen, Julia Hoffmann.
items.en: At the end I write: Kind regards, Julia Hoffmann.
items.note: Informally the same email ends with Viele Grüße or Liebe Grüße plus your first name only. Note the ß in Grüßen after a long vowel.

activities.title: Förmlich oder freundschaftlich?
activities.intro: Practise choosing the opening, the address form and the sign-off that match the reader.

questions.prompt: You are writing to a manager you have never met. Which opening is correct?
questions.explanation: Sehr geehrte plus surname is the standard formal opening. Liebe Frau Weber is warm and only fits someone you already know well.

questions.prompt: You are writing to your friend Anna about the weekend. Which sign-off fits?
questions.explanation: Liebe Grüße and Viele Grüße are the friendly closings. Mit freundlichen Grüßen belongs in business mail and Hochachtungsvoll is very stiff and old fashioned.

questions.prompt: Complete the formal opening to a woman whose surname you know: 'Sehr ___ Frau Weber, ich habe eine Frage.'
questions.explanation: The adjective takes the feminine ending: Sehr geehrte Frau Weber, but Sehr geehrter Herr Weber.

questions.prompt: Anna is a close friend. Complete her message so it stays familiar throughout: 'Kannst ___ mir bitte den Termin schicken?'
questions.explanation: Kannst is the second person singular form, so the pronoun must be du. Switching to Sie inside a friendly email is the most common register mistake.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am writing to you about your email of yesterday.' (formal)
questions.explanation: Schreiben takes a dative person, so Ihnen. Wegen governs the genitive, and die E-Mail becomes Ihrer E-Mail.

activities.title: Wie schreibe ich das?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend is unsure how formal an email should be, and you talk them through the opening, the address form and the sign-off.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich muss der Vermieterin schreiben. Wie fange ich an?
lines.en: I have to write to the landlady. How do I start?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Schreib: Sehr geehrte Frau Weber, ich schreibe Ihnen wegen der Heizung.
lines.en: Write: Dear Ms Weber, I am writing to you about the heating.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wenn ich dir schreibe? Auch so förmlich?
lines.en: And when I write to you? Just as formally?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, an mich schreibst du einfach: Hallo, meine Heizung ist kaputt.
lines.en: No, to me you simply write: Hi, my heating is broken.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und am Ende? Was schreibe ich der Vermieterin?
lines.en: And at the end? What do I write to the landlady?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Ende der förmlichen E-Mail steht: Mit freundlichen Grüßen und dein voller Name.
lines.en: At the end of the formal email you put: Kind regards and your full name.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und bei dir?
lines.en: And with you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bei mir reichen Liebe Grüße und dein Vorname.
lines.en: With me Liebe Grüße and your first name are enough.

days.title: Telefonieren, sich melden und weiterverbinden
days.objective: Answer the phone the German way, ask to be put through to a named person, understand that they are unavailable, and leave a message with a callback number.
days.paretoFocus: The telephone is the hardest everyday situation in a foreign language because there is no face to read and no chance to guess from context, so a small set of fixed phrases carries almost the whole call.
drills: Say your own surname aloud followed by guten Tag, ten times, until it sounds automatic.
Read your phone number aloud digit by digit in German, three times, at normal speed.
Act out both sides of a short call in which the person you want is not available and you leave a message.

items: How do people in Germany usually identify themselves when they pick up the phone?
Which polite question asks to be connected to a particular man?
What does a colleague say to offer to take a message, and how do you answer it?

activities.title: Am Telefon
activities.intro: A German phone call has a fixed shape. Both sides usually give their surname straight away, requests are softened with the polite Konjunktiv II, and the person who cannot help you offers to take a message. Learn these lines as whole blocks, because on the phone there is no time to build sentences from scratch.

items.hr: Hoffmann, guten Tag.
items.en: Hoffmann speaking, good day.
items.note: People in Germany answer with their surname, at work and often at home too. Just Hallo sounds unhelpful to the caller.

items.hr: Könnte ich bitte Herrn Braun sprechen?
items.en: Could I please speak to Mr Braun?
items.note: Könnte is the polite Konjunktiv II. Der Herr adds an n in every case except the nominative, so it is Herrn Braun here.

items.hr: Einen Moment bitte, ich verbinde Sie.
items.en: One moment please, I will put you through.
items.note: Der Moment. Verbinden means to connect, and the receptionist says this while transferring the call.

items.hr: Er ist gerade nicht am Platz. Kann ich etwas ausrichten?
items.en: He is not at his desk at the moment. Can I take a message?
items.note: Der Platz here means the desk or workstation. Ausrichten is separable, so the prefix goes to the end of the sentence.

items.hr: Ja gern, ich hinterlasse Ihnen meine Nummer: null eins sieben null, vier fünf sechs.
items.en: Yes please, I will leave you my number: zero one seven zero, four five six.
items.note: Die Nummer. Phone numbers are read digit by digit, and null is the German zero.

activities.title: Der Anruf im Büro
activities.intro: Practise the fixed phrases that carry a German phone call from the first second to the last.

questions.prompt: Which is the most typical way to pick up the phone in Germany?
questions.explanation: Giving your surname first is the standard. Asking wer ist da sounds suspicious and wollen Sie sounds rude.

questions.prompt: The person you asked for has stepped away from their desk. What does the colleague say?
options: Er ist gerade nicht am Platz.
questions.answer: Er ist gerade nicht am Platz.
questions.explanation: Am Platz sein is the fixed phrase for being at your desk. Keinen Platz haben means to have no room, which is something else entirely.

questions.prompt: The receptionist is transferring your call. Complete: 'Einen Moment bitte, ich ___ Sie.'
questions.explanation: Verbinden means to connect two parties. First person singular drops the infinitive ending: ich verbinde.

questions.prompt: The colleague offers to pass on what you say. Complete the separable verb: 'Kann ich etwas ___?'
questions.explanation: In a modal sentence the separable verb stays whole in the infinitive at the end, so ausrichten is not split here.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Could I please speak to Mr Braun?'
questions.explanation: A yes or no question puts the conjugated verb first, and the infinitive sprechen closes the sentence. Herrn carries the n ending in the accusative.

activities.title: Er ist nicht am Platz
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You call a company, ask for a named colleague, learn that he is away and leave a callback number.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Firma Braun und Partner, Schneider, guten Tag.
lines.en: Braun and Partner, Schneider speaking, good day.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Guten Tag, Frau Schneider. Könnte ich bitte Herrn Braun sprechen?
lines.en: Good day, Ms Schneider. Could I please speak to Mr Braun?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Einen Moment bitte, ich verbinde Sie.
lines.en: One moment please, I will put you through.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vielen Dank.
lines.en: Thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Es tut mir leid, er ist gerade nicht am Platz. Kann ich etwas ausrichten?
lines.en: I am sorry, he is not at his desk at the moment. Can I take a message?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja gern. Könnte er mich heute Nachmittag zurückrufen?
lines.en: Yes please. Could he call me back this afternoon?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Natürlich. Wie ist Ihre Nummer?
lines.en: Of course. What is your number?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Null eins sieben null, vier fünf sechs, zwei drei. Vielen Dank und auf Wiederhören.
lines.en: Zero one seven zero, four five six, two three. Thank you and goodbye.

days.title: Eine Einladung annehmen oder absagen
days.objective: Accept an invitation warmly, turn one down without causing offence, and propose a different time so the friendship survives the no.
days.paretoFocus: Learners master the yes and skip the no, but a bare nein ends invitations for good, while a refusal plus a reason plus an alternative keeps them coming.
drills: Write three different warm acceptances to the same invitation to dinner.
Write a refusal with a reason introduced by weil, then add a sentence proposing another day.
Say aloud five reasons you might genuinely have to decline something this month, each as a full weil clause.

items: Which two short phrases accept an invitation with warmth rather than just agreement?
Where does the verb go in a reason clause introduced by weil?
Which word introduces an alternative you offer after saying no?

activities.title: Ja gern oder leider nicht
activities.intro: A German refusal is expected to have three parts: a warm reaction, the word leider with a reason, and an alternative. Leider is what makes a no sound regretful rather than cold. The reason usually comes as a weil clause, which sends the conjugated verb to the very end.

items.hr: Wir feiern am Samstag meinen Geburtstag. Hast du Lust zu kommen?
items.en: We are celebrating my birthday on Saturday. Do you feel like coming?
items.note: Der Geburtstag. Lust haben zu plus infinitive is the everyday way to invite someone.

items.hr: Sehr gern! Ich komme auf jeden Fall.
items.en: I would love to! I will definitely come.
items.note: Der Fall. Auf jeden Fall means in any case and makes the yes sound firm and pleased.

items.hr: Das ist lieb von dir, aber leider kann ich am Samstag nicht.
items.en: That is kind of you, but unfortunately I cannot on Saturday.
items.note: Start with the warm reaction, then leider. Aber is a coordinating conjunction and does not move the verb.

items.hr: Ich muss leider absagen, weil ich am Wochenende arbeiten muss.
items.en: Unfortunately I have to cancel, because I have to work at the weekend.
items.note: Absagen is separable but stays whole after muss. Weil pushes the conjugated verb muss to the end.

items.hr: Wollen wir uns stattdessen am Sonntag treffen?
items.en: Shall we meet on Sunday instead?
items.note: Stattdessen means instead of that and signals the alternative. Sich treffen is reflexive: wir treffen uns.

activities.title: Zusagen und absagen
activities.intro: Practise saying yes with warmth and saying no with a reason and an alternative.

questions.prompt: A friend invites you and you are delighted. Which reply fits best?
options: Sehr gern, ich komme auf jeden Fall.
questions.answer: Sehr gern, ich komme auf jeden Fall.
questions.explanation: Sehr gern carries the warmth and auf jeden Fall confirms it. The other three all sound reluctant.

questions.prompt: Which sentence gives a reason correctly after a refusal?
options: weil ich am Samstag arbeiten muss
weil ich muss am Samstag arbeiten
weil muss ich am Samstag arbeiten
weil am Samstag ich arbeiten muss
questions.answer: weil ich am Samstag arbeiten muss
questions.explanation: Weil is a subordinating conjunction, so the conjugated verb muss goes last and the infinitive arbeiten sits just in front of it.

questions.prompt: You cannot make it after all. Complete with the separable verb meaning to call something off: 'Es tut mir leid, ich muss ___.'
questions.explanation: Absagen is the counterpart of zusagen. After the modal muss it stays as one unsplit infinitive at the end.

questions.prompt: Saturday is impossible, so you offer another day. Complete: 'Wollen wir uns ___ am Sonntag treffen?'
questions.explanation: Stattdessen refers back to the rejected plan and introduces the replacement. Without it the question sounds like a separate second invitation.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Unfortunately I cannot come because I have to work on Saturday.'
questions.explanation: Leider in first position pushes kann into second place. In the weil clause the conjugated muss closes the sentence.

activities.title: Am Samstag geht es leider nicht
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are invited to a barbecue, cannot make the day offered, and propose a different one.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wir grillen am Samstag im Garten. Hast du Lust zu kommen?
lines.en: We are having a barbecue in the garden on Saturday. Do you feel like coming?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das ist lieb von dir, aber am Samstag muss ich leider arbeiten.
lines.en: That is kind of you, but unfortunately I have to work on Saturday.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schade! Und am Sonntag?
lines.en: What a pity! And on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sonntag passt mir gut. Wollen wir uns stattdessen am Sonntag treffen?
lines.en: Sunday suits me well. Shall we meet on Sunday instead?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sehr gern. Kommst du um vier?
lines.en: Gladly. Are you coming at four?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, um vier komme ich auf jeden Fall. Soll ich einen Salat mitbringen?
lines.en: Yes, at four I will definitely come. Should I bring a salad?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das wäre super. Bis Sonntag!
lines.en: That would be great. See you Sunday!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bis Sonntag, ich freue mich!
lines.en: See you Sunday, I am looking forward to it!

days.title: Glückwünsche und Anteilnahme
days.objective: Say the right fixed phrase for a birthday, a wedding, an illness and a death, and know when it is too early to say it.
days.paretoFocus: These are short, frozen phrases that never change, so a handful of them covers every emotional moment you will share with German speakers for years.
drills: Write the correct phrase for four situations: a birthday, a new job, a flu, a funeral.
Say aloud one full sentence wishing someone well for something specific, using wünschen plus dative.
List the birthdays you know this year and note on which exact date you are allowed to send the message.

items: Which phrase congratulates someone on a birthday, and which preposition and case follow it?
What do you say to someone who is ill, and what do you say to someone who has lost a relative?
When is the earliest you should congratulate someone on their birthday in Germany?

activities.title: Herzlichen Glückwunsch und gute Besserung
activities.intro: German good wishes are fixed formulas that you learn whole rather than build. Most congratulations use zu plus the dative for the occasion, and wünschen takes a dative person plus an accusative thing. There is also a real custom to respect: congratulating before the birthday itself is considered bad luck.

items.hr: Herzlichen Glückwunsch zum Geburtstag!
items.en: Happy birthday!
items.note: Der Glückwunsch. Zu plus dative gives zum Geburtstag, zur Hochzeit, zum neuen Job.

items.hr: Ich wünsche dir alles Gute für deine neue Stelle.
items.en: I wish you all the best for your new job.
items.note: Die Stelle. Wünschen takes the person in the dative, dir, and the thing in the accusative.

items.hr: Gute Besserung! Ich hoffe, du wirst schnell wieder gesund.
items.en: Get well soon! I hope you get better quickly.
items.note: Die Besserung. This is the only phrase used for illness, from a cold to a hospital stay.

items.hr: Mein herzliches Beileid.
items.en: My sincere condolences.
items.note: Das Beileid. Said quietly and once, to someone who has lost a family member. Nothing more is expected of you.

items.hr: In Deutschland gratuliert man nicht vor dem Geburtstag, das bringt angeblich Unglück.
items.en: In Germany people do not congratulate before the birthday, it supposedly brings bad luck.
items.note: Das Unglück. Wait for the day itself, even if you will not see the person then.

activities.title: Das richtige Wort im richtigen Moment
activities.intro: Practise picking the one fixed phrase that fits each occasion.

questions.prompt: Your colleague is at home with the flu. What do you write?
questions.explanation: Gute Besserung is the only wish used for illness. Viel Erfolg is for exams and challenges.

questions.prompt: A friend has a birthday tomorrow. What is the German custom?
options: Man gratuliert erst am Geburtstag selbst.
questions.answer: Man gratuliert erst am Geburtstag selbst.
questions.explanation: Congratulating early is traditionally thought to bring bad luck, and many people react quite strongly to it.

questions.prompt: Complete the standard birthday wish: 'Herzlichen ___ zum Geburtstag!'
questions.explanation: Der Glückwunsch is masculine and takes the accusative ending on the adjective, which gives herzlichen.

questions.prompt: Someone tells you their father has died. Complete the fixed phrase: 'Mein herzliches ___.'
questions.explanation: Das Beileid is neuter, so the adjective takes the ending es. The phrase is never expanded or improvised.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I wish you all the best for your new job.'
questions.explanation: Dir is the dative person and alles Gute the accusative thing. Für takes the accusative, so deine neue Stelle.

activities.title: Was sagt man da?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks you which phrase to use in three different situations, including when it is still too early to say anything.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Morgen hat Lena Geburtstag. Soll ich ihr heute schon gratulieren?
lines.en: Lena has her birthday tomorrow. Should I congratulate her today already?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, in Deutschland gratuliert man erst am Geburtstag selbst.
lines.en: No, in Germany you only congratulate on the birthday itself.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut zu wissen. Und was sage ich dann?
lines.en: Good to know. And what do I say then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sag einfach: Herzlichen Glückwunsch zum Geburtstag, alles Gute!
lines.en: Just say: Happy birthday, all the best!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und Herrn Weber geht es schlecht, er liegt im Krankenhaus.
lines.en: And Mr Weber is unwell, he is in hospital.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann wünsch ihm gute Besserung, das freut ihn bestimmt.
lines.en: Then wish him a quick recovery, that will certainly please him.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Seine Mutter ist letzte Woche gestorben.
lines.en: His mother died last week.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das tut mir sehr leid. Da sagt man: Mein herzliches Beileid.
lines.en: I am very sorry. In that case you say: My sincere condolences.

days.title: Feste und Feiertage in Deutschland
days.objective: Name the main German public holidays with their dates, describe briefly what happens on each, and plan around the fact that shops close on Sundays and holidays.
days.paretoFocus: Closed shops on Sundays and public holidays shape the German week more than any other cultural fact, and a learner who misses it goes hungry at least once.
drills: Write the four holiday dates from this lesson into your own calendar for the coming year.
Describe in three German sentences what your household does on one holiday you celebrate.
Plan a full week of shopping in German out loud, saying on which day you buy what and why.

items: Which date is the German national holiday and what is it called?
What is the situation with shops on a Sunday or a public holiday?
After deshalb, where does the conjugated verb stand?

activities.title: Der Kalender in Deutschland
activities.intro: Germany has a handful of national public holidays plus regional ones, and on all of them shops stay shut. So do they every Sunday. The connector deshalb introduces the consequence and counts as the first element, so the conjugated verb follows immediately after it.

items.hr: Am 24. Dezember feiern die meisten Familien Heiligabend zu Hause.
items.en: On 24 December most families celebrate Christmas Eve at home.
items.note: Der Heiligabend. The presents are given on the evening of the 24th, not on the morning of the 25th.

items.hr: Ostern fällt in den Frühling, und der Ostermontag ist auch ein Feiertag.
items.en: Easter falls in spring, and Easter Monday is a public holiday too.
items.note: Der Feiertag, der Ostermontag. Both the Friday before and the Monday after Easter are free days.

items.hr: Der 3. Oktober ist der Tag der Deutschen Einheit, der Nationalfeiertag.
items.en: 3 October is the Day of German Unity, the national holiday.
items.note: Die Einheit. This is the day that marks the reunification of the country in 1990.

items.hr: Der 1. Mai heißt Tag der Arbeit und ist in ganz Deutschland frei.
items.en: 1 May is called Labour Day and is a day off across the whole of Germany.
items.note: Die Arbeit. Note heißt with ß after a diphthong.

items.hr: An Sonntagen und an Feiertagen sind fast alle Geschäfte geschlossen, deshalb kaufe ich samstags ein.
items.en: On Sundays and public holidays almost all shops are closed, so I do my shopping on Saturdays.
items.note: Das Geschäft. After deshalb the verb comes straight away: deshalb kaufe ich. Einkaufen is separable.

activities.title: Feiertage und Öffnungszeiten
activities.intro: Practise the holiday names, the dates, and the consequences for everyday planning.

questions.prompt: Which date is the German national holiday?
questions.explanation: The 3rd of October is the Tag der Deutschen Einheit. The 1st of May is a holiday too, but it is Labour Day.

questions.prompt: It is Sunday morning and you have no bread at home. What is true in most of Germany?
options: Die Geschäfte öffnen erst um 20 Uhr.
Nur am Sonntag sind alle Läden offen.
questions.explanation: Sunday closing is the rule. Only stations, airports and some bakeries in the morning are exceptions.

questions.prompt: Complete with the connector of consequence that keeps the verb in second position: 'Sonntags sind die Läden zu, ___ kaufe ich am Samstag ein.'
questions.explanation: Deshalb fills the first slot in the clause, so the conjugated verb kaufe has to follow it directly.

questions.prompt: Complete the name of the holiday on the first of May: 'Der 1. Mai ist der Tag der ___.'
questions.explanation: Die Arbeit stands in the genitive after der Tag, and feminine nouns take no genitive ending, so the form does not change.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'On public holidays almost all shops are closed.'
questions.explanation: An takes the dative for time, and the plural dative adds n, giving an Feiertagen. The verb sind stays in second position.

activities.title: Am Freitag ist frei
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend wants to go shopping on a public holiday and you explain the German calendar to them.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Am Freitag ist ein Feiertag, hast du frei?
lines.en: There is a public holiday on Friday, do you have the day off?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, der 3. Oktober ist der Tag der Deutschen Einheit, da arbeitet fast niemand.
lines.en: Yes, 3 October is the Day of German Unity, almost nobody works then.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dann können wir am Freitag zusammen einkaufen gehen, oder?
lines.en: Then we can go shopping together on Friday, right?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Leider nicht, an Feiertagen sind fast alle Geschäfte geschlossen.
lines.en: Unfortunately not, on public holidays almost all shops are closed.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und am Sonntag danach?
lines.en: And on the Sunday after?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sonntags auch nicht. Deshalb kaufe ich immer am Samstag ein.
lines.en: Not on Sundays either. That is why I always shop on Saturday.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut, dann gehen wir am Freitag lieber spazieren.
lines.en: Fine, then we would rather go for a walk on Friday.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sehr gern, und danach koche ich für uns.
lines.en: Gladly, and afterwards I will cook for us.

days.title: Handy, Internet und soziale Medien
days.objective: Talk about your phone and being online: charging it, poor reception, sending and answering messages, and switching the thing off.
days.paretoFocus: Half of all everyday excuses and half of all everyday arrangements now run through a phone, so these few separable verbs appear in more real conversations than any grammar point.
drills: Describe your own phone habits in five German sentences, using aufladen, ausschalten and schicken at least once each.
Write three excuses in the perfect tense for why you did not answer a message.
Say aloud where in your daily route the reception is bad, using kaum Empfang.

items: Which separable verb do you use when the battery is empty, and where does its prefix go?
How do you say that you have almost no signal?
How do you form the perfect of schicken and of schreiben?

activities.title: Mein Handy und ich
activities.intro: Talking about phones runs almost entirely on separable verbs: aufladen, ausschalten, anrufen, einschalten. In a main clause the prefix jumps to the end of the sentence, and in the perfect it wraps around the ge, as in aufgeladen. Learn the nouns with their articles, because half of them are neuter.

items.hr: Mein Handy ist fast leer, ich muss es aufladen.
items.en: My phone is almost empty, I have to charge it.
items.note: Das Handy. Leer sein is what Germans say about a battery. After the modal muss the verb stays whole.

items.hr: Hier ist das Netz schlecht, ich habe kaum Empfang.
items.en: The network is bad here, I have hardly any signal.
items.note: Das Netz, der Empfang. Kaum means hardly and is stronger and more natural than nicht viel here.

items.hr: Kannst du mir die Adresse als Nachricht schicken?
items.en: Can you send me the address as a message?
items.note: Die Adresse, die Nachricht. Schicken takes the person in the dative, mir, and the thing in the accusative.

items.hr: Abends schalte ich das Handy aus, dann schlafe ich besser.
items.en: In the evenings I switch the phone off, then I sleep better.
items.note: Ausschalten is separable, so aus lands at the end of the clause.

items.hr: Ich habe dir gestern geschrieben, aber du hast nicht geantwortet.
items.en: I wrote to you yesterday, but you did not answer.
items.note: Schreiben is irregular: geschrieben. Antworten is regular and takes a dative person: mir antworten.

activities.title: Online und offline
activities.intro: Practise the separable verbs and the vocabulary of phones, networks and messages.

questions.prompt: Your battery is nearly empty. What do you say?
questions.explanation: Aufladen is to charge. Einladen means to invite and hochladen means to upload, which is a different action entirely.

questions.prompt: You are in a cellar and there is no signal. Which sentence fits?
questions.explanation: Der Empfang is the reception, so the negative article takes the masculine accusative form keinen.

questions.prompt: In the cinema you always turn the device off. Complete with the separable prefix: 'Im Kino schalte ich mein Handy immer ___.'
questions.explanation: In a main clause the prefix of ausschalten separates from the verb and moves to the very end.

questions.prompt: Complete in the perfect tense: 'Ich habe dir gestern eine Nachricht ___.'
questions.explanation: Schicken is regular, so the participle is ge plus stem plus t. It closes the sentence after the auxiliary habe.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Can you send me the address as a message?'
questions.explanation: The modal is conjugated and comes first in the question, the dative pronoun mir precedes the accusative object, and the infinitive schicken closes the sentence.

activities.title: Warum hast du nicht geantwortet?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend is annoyed that you did not reply, and you explain using the perfect tense and the phone vocabulary from this lesson.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich habe dir gestern Abend geschrieben, aber du hast nicht geantwortet.
lines.en: I wrote to you yesterday evening, but you did not answer.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Entschuldigung, mein Handy war leer und ich konnte es nicht aufladen.
lines.en: Sorry, my phone was empty and I could not charge it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und heute Morgen?
lines.en: And this morning?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Heute Morgen war ich im Zug, da hatte ich kaum Empfang.
lines.en: This morning I was on the train, I had hardly any signal there.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kein Problem. Schickst du mir die Adresse als Nachricht?
lines.en: No problem. Will you send me the address as a message?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja klar, ich schicke sie dir sofort.
lines.en: Yes of course, I will send it to you right away.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super. Bist du heute Abend online?
lines.en: Great. Are you online this evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, abends schalte ich das Handy aus, dann schlafe ich besser.
lines.en: No, in the evenings I switch the phone off, then I sleep better.

days.title: Seine Meinung sagen und einen Vorschlag machen
days.objective: State an opinion with three different openers, make a suggestion two ways, and agree or disagree without sounding blunt.
days.paretoFocus: This is the doorway to real conversation: once you can say what you think and propose what to do, you stop only answering questions and start steering the exchange.
drills: Give your opinion on three everyday questions, using a different opener each time.
Turn each opinion into a suggestion using ich schlage vor and then using wollen wir.
Practise disagreeing politely three times, always starting with something you accept in the other view.

items: Which case does meiner Meinung nach require, and where does the verb go after it?
What happens to the verb in a clause introduced by dass?
How do you soften a disagreement before you state your own view?

activities.title: Ich finde, ich glaube, ich schlage vor
activities.intro: Opinions in German open with a small set of phrases. Ich finde and ich glaube are followed by a comma and a normal clause with the verb in second position, while ich glaube, dass sends the verb to the end. Meiner Meinung nach is a fixed phrase in the dative, and because it fills the first slot the conjugated verb has to come straight after it.

items.hr: Ich finde, das ist eine gute Idee.
items.en: I think that is a good idea.
items.note: Die Idee. After the comma the clause keeps normal word order with ist in second position.

items.hr: Meiner Meinung nach ist das Wetter zu schlecht für einen Ausflug.
items.en: In my opinion the weather is too bad for an excursion.
items.note: Die Meinung takes the dative here, meiner Meinung, and nach follows the noun. The verb ist comes immediately after the phrase.

items.hr: Ich glaube, dass wir früher losfahren sollten.
items.en: I think we should set off earlier.
items.note: Dass sends the conjugated verb sollten to the very end, after the infinitive losfahren.

items.hr: Ich schlage vor, wir treffen uns um sieben.
items.en: I suggest we meet at seven.
items.note: Der Vorschlag, vorschlagen. The prefix vor separates and closes the first clause.

items.hr: Da hast du Recht, aber ich sehe das ein bisschen anders.
items.en: You are right there, but I see it a little differently.
items.note: Concede first, then disagree. A bare nein or das stimmt nicht sounds harsh in German conversation.

activities.title: Meinung, Vorschlag, Widerspruch
activities.intro: Practise stating a view, proposing a plan, and disagreeing politely.

questions.prompt: Which fixed phrase for giving an opinion is grammatically correct?
questions.explanation: Nach governs the dative, so die Meinung becomes meiner Meinung, and in this phrase nach stands after the noun rather than before it.

questions.prompt: You want to propose going for a walk. Which suggestion is correct?
options: Wie wäre es mit einem Spaziergang?
Wie wäre es mit ein Spaziergang?
Wie wäre es für einen Spaziergang?
questions.answer: Wie wäre es mit einem Spaziergang?
questions.explanation: Wie wäre es mit is always followed by the dative, and der Spaziergang gives einem Spaziergang.

questions.prompt: Soften your objection before stating your own view. Complete: 'Da hast du Recht, aber ich sehe das ein bisschen ___.'
questions.explanation: Anders is the adverb meaning differently and never takes an ending. Andere would be an adjective before a noun.

questions.prompt: You are putting an idea on the table. Complete with the separable prefix: 'Ich schlage ___, dass wir uns um sieben treffen.'
questions.explanation: Vorschlagen splits in a main clause, so vor moves to the end of that clause, right before the comma.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I think that we should set off earlier.'
questions.explanation: Dass is subordinating, so the conjugated sollten goes last, behind the infinitive losfahren.

activities.title: Was schlägst du vor?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend disagree about a weekend plan, and you use opinion openers and suggestion phrases to reach a decision.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sollen wir am Wochenende ans Meer fahren?
lines.en: Shall we drive to the sea at the weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Meiner Meinung nach ist das zu weit für zwei Tage.
lines.en: In my opinion that is too far for two days.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Da hast du vielleicht Recht. Was schlägst du vor?
lines.en: You may be right there. What do you suggest?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich schlage vor, wir bleiben hier. Wie wäre es mit einem Ausflug an den See?
lines.en: I suggest we stay here. How about a trip to the lake?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich weiß nicht, ich finde den See ein bisschen langweilig.
lines.en: I do not know, I find the lake a little boring.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich glaube, dass wir dort trotzdem einen schönen Tag haben können.
lines.en: I think that we can still have a lovely day there.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut, du hast mich überzeugt. Wollen wir früh losfahren?
lines.en: Fine, you have convinced me. Shall we set off early?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich finde, um acht ist perfekt.
lines.en: Yes, I think eight o'clock is perfect.
